infobox.value: ([US](/wiki/American_English "American English"): [/ˈmɛrɪlənd/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-us-Maryland.ogg "File:En-us-Maryland.ogg"))[[b]](#cite_note-10) is a [state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") in the [Mid-Atlantic](/wiki/Mid-Atlantic_(United_States) "Mid-Atlantic (United States)")[[9]](#cite_note-11) and [Southern](https://www2.census.gov/geo/pdfs/maps-data/maps/reference/us_regdiv.pdf) [[10]](#cite_note-12) regions of the United States. It borders [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia") to its south, [West Virginia](/wiki/West_Virginia "West Virginia") to its west, [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania") to its north, and [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware "Delaware") to its east, as well as with the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") to its east, and the national capital and federal district of [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.") to the southwest. With a total area of 12,407 square miles (32,130 km2), Maryland is the [ninth-smallest state by land area](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_area "List of U.S. states and territories by area"),[[11]](#cite_note-13) and its population of 6.1 million ranks it the [19th-most populous state](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_te

**Maryland** ([US](/wiki/American_English "American English"): [/ˈmɛrɪlənd/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-us-Maryland.ogg "File:En-us-Maryland.ogg"))[[b]](#cite_note-10) is a [state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") in the [Mid-Atlantic](/wiki/Mid-Atlantic_(United_States) "Mid-Atlantic (United States)")[[9]](#cite_note-11) and [Southern](https://www2.census.gov/geo/pdfs/maps-data/maps/reference/us_regdiv.pdf) [[10]](#cite_note-12) regions of the United States. It borders [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia") to its south, [West Virginia](/wiki/West_Virginia "West Virginia") to its west, [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania") to its north, and [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware "Delaware") to its east, as well as with the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") to its east, and the national capital and federal district of [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.") to the southwest. With a total area of 12,407 square miles (32,130 km2), Maryland is the [ninth-smallest state by land area](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_area "List of U.S. states and territories by area"),[[11]](#cite_note-13) and its population of 6.1 million ranks it the [19th-most populous state](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_population "List of U.S. states and territories by population") and the [fifth-most densely populated](/wiki/List_of_states_and_territories_of_the_United_States_by_population_density "List of states and territories of the United States by population density"). Maryland's capital city is [Annapolis](/wiki/Annapolis,_Maryland "Annapolis, Maryland"), and the state's most populous city is [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore").[[12]](#cite_note-BaltBrit-14)

Maryland's coastline was first explored by Europeans in the 16th century. Prior to that, it was inhabited by several [Native American](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") tribes, mostly the [Algonquian peoples](/wiki/Algonquian_peoples "Algonquian peoples").[[13]](#cite_note-15) One of the original [Thirteen Colonies](/wiki/Thirteen_Colonies "Thirteen Colonies"), the [Province of Maryland](/wiki/Province_of_Maryland "Province of Maryland") was founded in 1634 by [George Calvert, 1st Baron Baltimore](/wiki/George_Calvert,_1st_Baron_Baltimore "George Calvert, 1st Baron Baltimore")[*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:Maryland#Dubious "Talk:Maryland")*], who sought to provide a religious haven for Catholics persecuted in England.[[14]](#cite_note-:1-16) Religious strife was common in Maryland's early years, and [Catholics](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") remained a minority, albeit in greater numbers than in any other English colony. Its economy was heavily [plantation-based](/wiki/Plantation_economy "Plantation economy") and centered mostly on the cultivation of [tobacco](/wiki/Tobacco "Tobacco"). Demand for cheap labor from Maryland colonists led to the importation of numerous [indentured servants](/wiki/Indentured_servitude_in_British_America "Indentured servitude in British America") and [enslaved Africans](/wiki/History_of_slavery_in_Maryland "History of slavery in Maryland"). In 1760, Maryland's current boundaries took form following the [settlement](/wiki/Mason%E2%80%93Dixon_line "Mason–Dixon line") of a long-running border dispute with Pennsylvania. Although it was a [slave state](/wiki/Slave_states_and_free_states "Slave states and free states"), Maryland [remained in the Union](/wiki/Border_states_(American_Civil_War) "Border states (American Civil War)") during the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), and its proximity to Washington D.C. and Virginia made it [a significant strategic location](/wiki/Maryland_in_the_American_Civil_War "Maryland in the American Civil War"). After the Civil War ended in 1865, Maryland took part in the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution_in_the_United_States "Industrial Revolution in the United States"), driven by its seaports, railroad networks, and mass immigration from Europe.

Since the 1940s, the state's population has grown rapidly, to approximately six million residents, and it is among the most densely populated U.S. states. As of 2015[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit), Maryland had the [highest median household income](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_by_income "List of U.S. states by income") of any state, owing in large part to its proximity to Washington, D.C., and a highly diversified economy spanning manufacturing, retail services, public administration, real estate, higher education, information technology, defense contracting, health care, and biotechnology.[[15]](#cite_note-17) Maryland is one of the most multicultural states in the country; it is one of the nine states where [non-Whites compose a majority of the population](/wiki/Majority_minority_in_the_United_States "Majority minority in the United States"), with the fifth-highest percentage of [African Americans](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans"), and high numbers of residents born in [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa"), [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"), [Central America](/wiki/Central_America "Central America"), and the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean"). The state's central role in U.S. history is reflected by its hosting of some of the [highest numbers of historic landmarks](/wiki/List_of_U.S._National_Historic_Landmarks_by_state "List of U.S. National Historic Landmarks by state") per capita.

The [western](/wiki/Western_Maryland "Western Maryland") portion of the state contains stretches of the [Appalachian Mountains](/wiki/Appalachian_Mountains "Appalachian Mountains"), the [central portion](/wiki/Baltimore_metropolitan_area "Baltimore metropolitan area") is primarily composed of the [Piedmont](/wiki/Piedmont_(United_States) "Piedmont (United States)"), and the [eastern side of the state](/wiki/Eastern_Shore_of_Maryland "Eastern Shore of Maryland") makes up a significant portion of the Chesapeake Bay. Sixteen of Maryland's twenty-three counties, and the city of Baltimore, border the tidal waters of the Chesapeake Bay estuary and its many tributaries,[[16]](#cite_note-18)[[12]](#cite_note-BaltBrit-14) which combined total more than 4,000 miles of shoreline. Although one of the smallest states in the U.S., it features a variety of climates and topographical features that have earned it the moniker of *America in Miniature*.[[17]](#cite_note-19) Maryland's geography, culture, and history are diverse, including elements of the Mid-Atlantic, [Northeastern](/wiki/Northeastern_United_States "Northeastern United States"), and Southern regions of the country.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main articles: [History of Maryland](/wiki/History_of_Maryland "History of Maryland") and [Native American tribes in Maryland](/wiki/Native_American_tribes_in_Maryland "Native American tribes in Maryland")

### 17th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 17th century")]

#### Maryland's first colonial settlement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Maryland's first colonial settlement")]

Main article: [Province of Maryland](/wiki/Province_of_Maryland "Province of Maryland")

Present-day Maryland was originally inhabited by tribes such as the [Piscataway](/wiki/Piscataway_people "Piscataway people") (including the [Patuxent](/wiki/Patuxent_people "Patuxent people")), the [Nanticoke](/wiki/Nanticoke_people "Nanticoke people") (including the [Tocwogh](/wiki/Tockwogh "Tockwogh"), the [Ozinie](/wiki/Ozinie "Ozinie") and other subdivisions), the [Powhatan](/wiki/Powhatan "Powhatan"), the [Lenape](/wiki/Lenape "Lenape"), the [Susquehannock](/wiki/Susquehannock "Susquehannock"), the [Shawnee](/wiki/Shawnee "Shawnee"), the [Tutelo](/wiki/Tutelo "Tutelo"), the [Saponi](/wiki/Saponi "Saponi"), the [Pocomoke](/wiki/Pocomoke_people "Pocomoke people") and the [Massawomeck](/wiki/Massawomeck_people "Massawomeck people").[[18]](#cite_note-20)[*[self-published source?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Self-published_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] [George Calvert, 1st Baron Baltimore](/wiki/George_Calvert,_1st_Baron_Baltimore "George Calvert, 1st Baron Baltimore") (1579–1632), sought a charter from King [Charles I](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England") for the territory between [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts") to the north and [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia") to the immediate south.[[19]](#cite_note-Stewart_1967_42–43-21)
After Baltimore died in April 1632, the charter was granted to his son, [Cecilius Calvert, 2nd Baron Baltimore](/wiki/Cecilius_Calvert,_2nd_Baron_Baltimore "Cecilius Calvert, 2nd Baron Baltimore") (1605–1675), on June 20, 1632. Officially, the new "Maryland Colony" was named in honor of [Henrietta Maria of France](/wiki/Henrietta_Maria_of_France "Henrietta Maria of France"), wife of Charles I.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarsh20115-22) Lord Baltimore initially proposed the name "Crescentia", the land of growth or increase, but "the King proposed Terra Mariae [Mary Land], which was concluded on and inserted in the bill."[[14]](#cite_note-:1-16)

The original capital of Maryland was [St. Mary's City](/wiki/St._Mary%27s_City,_Maryland "St. Mary's City, Maryland"), on the north shore of the [Potomac River](/wiki/Potomac_River "Potomac River"), and the county surrounding it was the first erected and created in the province,[[21]](#cite_note-23) first called Augusta Carolina, after the King, and later named [St. Mary's County](/wiki/St._Mary%27s_County,_Maryland "St. Mary's County, Maryland").[[22]](#cite_note-24)

Lord Baltimore's first settlers arrived in the new colony in March 1634, with his younger brother, [Leonard Calvert](/wiki/Leonard_Calvert "Leonard Calvert") (1606–1647), as the first provincial [Governor of Maryland](/wiki/Governor_of_Maryland "Governor of Maryland"). They made their first permanent settlement at [St. Mary's City](/wiki/St._Mary%27s_City,_Maryland "St. Mary's City, Maryland") in what is now [St. Mary's County](/wiki/St._Mary%27s_County,_Maryland "St. Mary's County, Maryland"). They purchased the site from the [paramount chief](/wiki/Paramount_chief "Paramount chief") of the region, who was eager to establish trade. St. Mary's became the first [capital](/wiki/State_capital "State capital") of Maryland, and remained so for 60 years until 1695. More settlers soon followed. Their tobacco crops were successful and quickly made the new colony profitable. However, given the incidence of [malaria](/wiki/Malaria "Malaria"), [yellow fever](/wiki/Yellow_fever "Yellow fever"), and [typhoid](/wiki/Typhoid "Typhoid"), life expectancy in Maryland was about 10 years less than in [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England").[[23]](#cite_note-iha-25)

#### Persecution of Catholics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Persecution of Catholics")]

See also: [Plundering Time](/wiki/Plundering_Time "Plundering Time")

Maryland was founded to provide a haven for England's [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") minority.[[24]](#cite_note-washingtonpost.com-26)
Although Maryland was the most heavily Catholic of the English mainland colonies, the religion was still in the minority, consisting of less than 10% of the total population.[[25]](#cite_note-:0-27)

In 1642, several [Puritans](/wiki/Puritans "Puritans") left [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia") for Maryland and founded the city of Providence, now called [Annapolis](/wiki/Annapolis,_Maryland "Annapolis, Maryland"), on the western shore of the upper [Chesapeake Bay](/wiki/Chesapeake_Bay "Chesapeake Bay").[[26]](#cite_note-28) A dispute with traders from Virginia over [Kent Island](/wiki/Kent_Island "Kent Island") in the Chesapeake led to armed conflict. In 1644, [William Claiborne](/wiki/William_Claiborne "William Claiborne"), a Puritan, seized Kent Island while his associate, the [pro-Parliament](/wiki/Roundheads "Roundheads") Puritan [Richard Ingle](/wiki/Richard_Ingle "Richard Ingle"), took over St. Mary's.[[27]](#cite_note-29) Both used religion as a tool to gain popular support. The two years from 1644 to 1646 when Claiborne and his Puritan associates held sway were known as "The Plundering Time". They captured Jesuit priests, imprisoned them, and then sent them back to England.

In 1646, [Leonard Calvert](/wiki/Leonard_Calvert "Leonard Calvert") returned with troops, recaptured St. Mary's City, and restored order. The House of Delegates passed the "Act concerning Religion" in 1649, granting religious liberty to all [Trinitarian](/wiki/Trinitarianism "Trinitarianism") Christians.[[23]](#cite_note-iha-25)

In 1650, the Puritans revolted against the proprietary government. "Protestants swept the Catholics out of the legislature ... and religious strife returned."[[23]](#cite_note-iha-25) The Puritans set up a new government prohibiting both [Roman Catholicism](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church "Roman Catholic Church") and [Anglicanism](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"). The Puritan revolutionary government persecuted Maryland Catholics during its reign, known as the "plundering time". Mobs burned down all the original Catholic churches of southern Maryland. The Puritan rule lasted until 1658, when the Calvert family and Lord Baltimore regained proprietary control and re-enacted the Toleration Act.

After England's [Glorious Revolution](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution "Glorious Revolution") in 1688, Maryland outlawed Catholicism. In 1704, the Maryland General Assembly prohibited Catholics from operating schools, limited the corporate ownership of property to hamper religious orders from expanding or supporting themselves, and encouraged the conversion of Catholic children.[[25]](#cite_note-:0-27) The celebration of the Catholic sacraments was also officially restricted. This state of affairs lasted until after the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War") (1775–1783). Wealthy Catholic planters built chapels on their land to practice their religion in relative secrecy.

Into the 18th century, individual priests and lay leaders claimed Maryland farms belonging to the Jesuits as personal property and [bequeathed](/wiki/Bequest "Bequest") them to evade the legal restrictions on religious organizations' owning property.[[25]](#cite_note-:0-27)

### Border disputes (1681–1760)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Border disputes (1681–1760)")]

Main articles: [Penn–Calvert boundary dispute](/wiki/Penn%E2%80%93Calvert_boundary_dispute "Penn–Calvert boundary dispute") and [Cresap's War](/wiki/Cresap%27s_War "Cresap's War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/1732_map_of_Maryland.jpg/250px-1732_map_of_Maryland.jpg)](/wiki/File:1732_map_of_Maryland.jpg)

A 1732 map of Maryland[[28]](#cite_note-30)

The royal charter granted Maryland the land north of the [Potomac River](/wiki/Potomac_River "Potomac River") up to the [40th parallel](/wiki/40th_parallel_north "40th parallel north"). A problem arose when [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England") granted a charter for the [Province of Pennsylvania](/wiki/Province_of_Pennsylvania "Province of Pennsylvania"), which defined Pennsylvania's southern border as the 40th parallel, identical to Maryland's northern border. But the grant indicated that Charles II and [William Penn](/wiki/William_Penn "William Penn") assumed the 40th parallel would pass close to [New Castle, Delaware](/wiki/New_Castle,_Delaware "New Castle, Delaware"), where it falls north of [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"), which Penn already designated as Pennsylvania's capital city. Negotiations ensued after the problem was discovered in 1681.

A compromise proposed by Charles II in 1682 was undermined by Penn's receiving the additional grant of what is now Delaware.[[29]](#cite_note-hubbard-31) Penn successfully argued that the Maryland charter entitled Lord Baltimore only to unsettled lands, and Dutch settlement in Delaware predated his charter. The dispute remained unresolved for nearly a century, carried on by the descendants of William Penn and Lord Baltimore—the [Calvert family](/wiki/Baron_Baltimore "Baron Baltimore"), which controlled Maryland, and the [Penn family](/wiki/William_Penn "William Penn"), which controlled Pennsylvania.[[29]](#cite_note-hubbard-31)

The border dispute with Pennsylvania led to Cresap's War in the 1730s. Hostilities erupted in 1730 and escalated through the first half of the decade, culminating in the deployment of military forces by Maryland in 1736 and by Pennsylvania in 1737. The armed phase of the conflict ended in May 1738 with the intervention of King George II, who compelled the negotiation of a cease-fire. A provisional agreement had been established in 1732.[[29]](#cite_note-hubbard-31)

Negotiations continued until a final agreement was signed in 1760. The agreement defined the border between Maryland and Pennsylvania as the line of latitude now known as the [Mason–Dixon line](/wiki/Mason%E2%80%93Dixon_line "Mason–Dixon line"). Maryland's border with Delaware was based on the [Transpeninsular Line](/wiki/Transpeninsular_Line "Transpeninsular Line") and the [Twelve-Mile Circle](/wiki/Twelve-Mile_Circle "Twelve-Mile Circle") around New Castle.[[29]](#cite_note-hubbard-31)

### 18th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 18th century")]

Main articles: [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), [Maryland in the American Revolution](/wiki/Maryland_in_the_American_Revolution "Maryland in the American Revolution"), [Lee Resolution](/wiki/Lee_Resolution "Lee Resolution"), [United States Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence"), [Philadelphia campaign](/wiki/Philadelphia_campaign "Philadelphia campaign"), [Articles of Confederation § Ratification](/wiki/Articles_of_Confederation#Ratification "Articles of Confederation"), [Treaty of Paris (1783)](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1783) "Treaty of Paris (1783)"), [Mount Vernon Conference](/wiki/Mount_Vernon_Conference "Mount Vernon Conference"), [Annapolis Convention (1786)](/wiki/Annapolis_Convention_(1786) "Annapolis Convention (1786)"), [Constitutional Convention (United States)](/wiki/Constitutional_Convention_(United_States) "Constitutional Convention (United States)"), [Admission to the Union](/wiki/Admission_to_the_Union "Admission to the Union"), and [List of U.S. states by date of admission to the Union](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_by_date_of_admission_to_the_Union "List of U.S. states by date of admission to the Union")

Most of the English colonists arrived in Maryland as [indentured servants](/wiki/Indentured_servant "Indentured servant"), and had to serve a several-year term as laborers to pay for their passage.[[30]](#cite_note-32) In the early years, the line between indentured servants and African slaves or laborers was fluid, and white and black laborers commonly lived and worked together, and formed unions. [Mixed-race](/wiki/Mixed-race "Mixed-race") children born to white mothers were considered free by the principle of *[partus sequitur ventrem](/wiki/Partus_sequitur_ventrem "Partus sequitur ventrem")*, by which children took the social status of their mothers, a principle of slave law that was adopted throughout the colonies, following Virginia in 1662.

Many of the free black families migrated to Delaware, where land was cheaper.[[31]](#cite_note-Heinegg-33) As the flow of indentured laborers to the colony decreased with improving economic conditions in England, planters in Maryland imported thousands more slaves and racial caste lines hardened.

Maryland was one of the [thirteen colonies](/wiki/Thirteen_Colonies "Thirteen Colonies") that revolted against British rule in the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution"). Near the end of the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War") (1775–1783), on February 2, 1781, Maryland became the last and 13th state to approve the ratification of the [Articles of Confederation and Perpetual Union](/wiki/Articles_of_Confederation "Articles of Confederation"), first proposed in 1776 and adopted by the [Second Continental Congress](/wiki/Second_Continental_Congress "Second Continental Congress") in 1778, which brought into being the United States as a united, [sovereign](/wiki/Sovereignty "Sovereignty") and [national state](/wiki/Nation_state "Nation state"). It also became the seventh state admitted to the Union after ratifying the new federal Constitution in 1788. In December 1790, prior to the move of the national capital from [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") in 1800, Maryland donated land selected by first President [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington") to the [federal government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States") for its creation. The land was provided along the north shore of the [Potomac River](/wiki/Potomac_River "Potomac River") from [Montgomery](/wiki/Montgomery_County,_Maryland "Montgomery County, Maryland") and [Prince George's](/wiki/Prince_George%27s_County,_Maryland "Prince George's County, Maryland") counties, as well as from [Fairfax County](/wiki/Fairfax_County,_Virginia "Fairfax County, Virginia") and [Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria,_Virginia "Alexandria, Virginia") on the south shore of the Potomac in [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia"); however, the land donated by the Commonwealth of Virginia was later returned to that state by the [District of Columbia retrocession](/wiki/District_of_Columbia_retrocession "District of Columbia retrocession") in 1846.

### 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 19th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Ft._Henry_bombardement_1814.jpg/250px-Ft._Henry_bombardement_1814.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ft._Henry_bombardement_1814.jpg)

The bombardment of Fort McHenry in the [Battle of Baltimore](/wiki/Battle_of_Baltimore "Battle of Baltimore"), which inspired "[The Star-Spangled Banner](/wiki/The_Star-Spangled_Banner "The Star-Spangled Banner")"

Influenced by a changing economy, revolutionary ideals, and preaching by ministers, numerous planters in Maryland freed their slaves in the 20 years after the [Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution"). Across the Upper South the free black population increased from less than 1% before the war to 14% by 1810.[[32]](#cite_note-Kolchin-34) Abolitionists [Harriet Tubman](/wiki/Harriet_Tubman "Harriet Tubman") and [Frederick Douglass](/wiki/Frederick_Douglass "Frederick Douglass") were born slaves during this time in [Dorchester County](/wiki/Dorchester_County,_Maryland "Dorchester County, Maryland") and [Talbot County](/wiki/Talbot_County,_Maryland "Talbot County, Maryland"), respectively.[[33]](#cite_note-35)[[34]](#cite_note-36)

During the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812"), the British military attempted to capture Baltimore, which was protected by [Fort McHenry](/wiki/Fort_McHenry "Fort McHenry"). During its bombardment, the song "[The Star-Spangled Banner](/wiki/The_Star-Spangled_Banner "The Star-Spangled Banner")" was written by [Francis Scott Key](/wiki/Francis_Scott_Key "Francis Scott Key"); it was later adopted as the national anthem.

National Road, later renamed [U.S. Route 40](/wiki/U.S._Route_40_in_Maryland "U.S. Route 40 in Maryland"), was authorized in 1817 as the federal highway, and ran from [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore") to [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis "St. Louis"). The [Baltimore and Ohio Railroad](/wiki/Baltimore_and_Ohio_Railroad "Baltimore and Ohio Railroad"), the first chartered railroad in the United States, opened its first section of track for regular operation in 1830 between Baltimore and [Ellicott City](/wiki/Ellicott_City,_Maryland "Ellicott City, Maryland"),[[35]](#cite_note-Dilts-37) and in 1852 it became the first rail line to reach the [Ohio River](/wiki/Ohio_River "Ohio River") from the eastern seaboard.[[36]](#cite_note-Stover-38)

#### Civil War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Civil War")]

Main article: [Maryland in the American Civil War](/wiki/Maryland_in_the_American_Civil_War "Maryland in the American Civil War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Kurz_%26_Allison_-_Battle_of_Antietam.jpg/250px-Kurz_%26_Allison_-_Battle_of_Antietam.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kurz_%26_Allison_-_Battle_of_Antietam.jpg)

The [Battle of Antietam](/wiki/Battle_of_Antietam "Battle of Antietam") in 1862, one of the bloodiest battles of the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), with nearly 23,000 casualties

The state remained in the Union during the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"),[[37]](#cite_note-39) due in significant part to demographics and Federal intervention. The 1860 census, held shortly before the outbreak of the civil war, showed that 49% of Maryland's African Americans were [free](/wiki/Free_black "Free black").[[32]](#cite_note-Kolchin-34)

Governor [Thomas Holliday Hicks](/wiki/Thomas_Holliday_Hicks "Thomas Holliday Hicks") suspended the state legislature, and to help ensure [the election](/wiki/1861_Maryland_gubernatorial_election "1861 Maryland gubernatorial election") of a new pro-union governor and legislature, President [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln") had a number of its pro-slavery politicians arrested, including the Mayor of Baltimore, [George William Brown](/wiki/George_William_Brown_(mayor) "George William Brown (mayor)"); suspended several civil liberties, including *habeas corpus*; and ordered artillery placed on [Federal Hill](/wiki/Federal_Hill,_Baltimore,_Maryland "Federal Hill, Baltimore, Maryland") overlooking Baltimore.

In April 1861, Federal units and state regiments were attacked as they marched through Baltimore, sparking the [Baltimore riot of 1861](/wiki/Baltimore_riot_of_1861 "Baltimore riot of 1861"), the first bloodshed in the Civil War.[[38]](#cite_note-40) The largest and most significant battle in the state was the [Battle of Antietam](/wiki/Battle_of_Antietam "Battle of Antietam") on September 17, 1862, near [Sharpsburg](/wiki/Sharpsburg,_Maryland "Sharpsburg, Maryland"). Although a tactical draw, the battle was considered a strategic Union victory and a [turning point](/wiki/Turning_point_of_the_American_Civil_War "Turning point of the American Civil War") of the war.

#### Post-Civil War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Post-Civil War")]

A new state constitution in 1864 [abolished](/wiki/Abolitionism_in_the_United_States "Abolitionism in the United States") slavery and Maryland was first recognized as a "Free State" in that context.[[39]](#cite_note-freestate-41) Following passage of constitutional amendments that granted voting rights to [freedmen](/wiki/Freedmen "Freedmen"), in 1867 the state extended suffrage to non-white males.

The [Democratic Party](/wiki/History_of_the_United_States_Democratic_Party "History of the United States Democratic Party") rapidly regained power in the state from [Republicans](/wiki/History_of_the_United_States_Republican_Party "History of the United States Republican Party"). Democrats replaced the Constitution of 1864 with the [Constitution of 1867](/wiki/Maryland_Constitution_of_1867 "Maryland Constitution of 1867"). Following the end of Reconstruction in 1877, Democrats devised means of [disenfranchising](/wiki/Disfranchisement_after_Reconstruction_era "Disfranchisement after Reconstruction era") blacks, initially by physical intimidation and voter fraud, later by constitutional amendments and laws. Blacks and immigrants, however, resisted Democratic Party disfranchisement efforts in the state. Maryland blacks were part of a biracial Republican coalition elected to state government in 1896–1904 and comprised 20% of the electorate.[[40]](#cite_note-tuck-42)

Compared to some other states, blacks were better established both before and after the civil war. Nearly half the black population was free before the war, and some had accumulated property. Half the population lived in cities. Literacy was high among blacks and, as Democrats crafted means to exclude them, suffrage campaigns helped reach blacks and teach them how to resist.[[40]](#cite_note-tuck-42) Whites did impose [racial segregation](/wiki/Racial_segregation "Racial segregation") in public facilities and [Jim Crow](/wiki/Jim_Crow "Jim Crow") laws, which effectively lasted until the passage of federal civil rights legislation in the mid-1960s.

Baltimore grew significantly during the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"), due in large part to its seaport and good railroad connections, attracting European immigrant labor. Many manufacturing businesses were established in the Baltimore area after the Civil War. Baltimore businessmen, including [Johns Hopkins](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins "Johns Hopkins"), [Enoch Pratt](/wiki/Enoch_Pratt "Enoch Pratt"), [George Peabody](/wiki/George_Peabody "George Peabody"), and [Henry Walters](/wiki/Henry_Walters "Henry Walters"), founded notable city institutions that bear their names, including respectively a [university](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_University "Johns Hopkins University"), [library system](/wiki/Enoch_Pratt_Free_Library "Enoch Pratt Free Library"), [music and dance conservatory](/wiki/Peabody_Institute "Peabody Institute"), and [art museum](/wiki/Walters_Art_Museum "Walters Art Museum").

[Cumberland](/wiki/Cumberland,_Maryland "Cumberland, Maryland") was Maryland's second-largest city in the 19th century. Nearby supplies of natural resources along with railroads fostered its growth into a major manufacturing center.[[41]](#cite_note-WDL-43)

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 20th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Rubble_of_the_Great_Baltimore_Fire.jpg/250px-Rubble_of_the_Great_Baltimore_Fire.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rubble_of_the_Great_Baltimore_Fire.jpg)

The ruins left by the [Great Baltimore Fire](/wiki/Great_Baltimore_Fire "Great Baltimore Fire") of 1904

The [Progressive Era](/wiki/Progressive_Era "Progressive Era") of the late 19th and early 20th centuries brought political reforms. In a series of laws passed between 1892 and 1908, reformers worked for standard state-issued ballots (rather than those distributed and marked by the parties); obtained closed voting booths to prevent party workers from "assisting" voters; initiated [primary elections](/wiki/Partisan_primary "Partisan primary") to keep party bosses from selecting candidates; and had candidates listed without party symbols, which discouraged the [illiterate](/wiki/Illiterate "Illiterate") from participating. These measures worked against ill-educated whites and blacks. The measures were led by the Democratic Party, which sought to disenfranchise Blacks, who were becoming an important part of the Republican Party.[[42]](#cite_note-44) Blacks resisted such efforts, with suffrage groups conducting voter education.
Blacks defeated three efforts to disenfranchise them, making alliances with immigrants to resist various Democratic campaigns.[[40]](#cite_note-tuck-42) Disenfranchisement bills in 1905, 1907, and 1911 were rebuffed, in large part because of black opposition. Blacks comprised 20% of the electorate and immigrants comprised 15%, and the legislature had difficulty devising requirements against blacks that did not also disadvantage immigrants.[[40]](#cite_note-tuck-42)

The Progressive Era also brought reforms in working conditions for Maryland's labor force. In 1902, the state regulated conditions in [mines](/wiki/Mining "Mining"); outlawed [child laborers](/wiki/Child_labor "Child labor") under the age of 12; mandated compulsory school attendance; and enacted the nation's first [workers' compensation](/wiki/Workers%27_compensation "Workers' compensation") law. The workers' compensation law was overturned in the courts, but was redrafted and finally enacted in 1910.

The [Great Baltimore Fire](/wiki/Great_Baltimore_Fire "Great Baltimore Fire") of 1904 burned for more than 30 hours, destroying 1,526 buildings and spanning 70 city blocks. More than 1,231 [firefighters](/wiki/Firefighter "Firefighter") worked to bring the blaze under control.

With the nation's [entry into World War I](/wiki/World_War_I#Entry_of_the_United_States "World War I") in 1917, new military bases such as [Camp Meade](/wiki/Fort_George_G._Meade "Fort George G. Meade"), the [Aberdeen Proving Ground](/wiki/Aberdeen_Proving_Ground "Aberdeen Proving Ground"), and the [Edgewood Arsenal](/wiki/Edgewood_Arsenal "Edgewood Arsenal") were established. Existing facilities, including [Fort McHenry](/wiki/Fort_McHenry "Fort McHenry"), were greatly expanded.

After Georgia congressman [William D. Upshaw](/wiki/William_D._Upshaw "William D. Upshaw") criticized Maryland openly in 1923 for not passing [Prohibition](/wiki/Prohibition_in_the_United_States "Prohibition in the United States") laws, *Baltimore Sun* editor Hamilton Owens coined the "Free State" nickname for Maryland in that context, which was popularized by [H. L. Mencken](/wiki/H._L._Mencken "H. L. Mencken") in a series of newspaper editorials.[[39]](#cite_note-freestate-41)[[43]](#cite_note-45)

Maryland's urban and rural communities had different experiences during the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression"). The "[Bonus Army](/wiki/Bonus_Army "Bonus Army")" marched through the state in 1932 on its way to Washington, D.C. Maryland instituted its first [income tax](/wiki/Income_tax "Income tax") in 1937 to generate revenue for schools and welfare.[[44]](#cite_note-46)

Passenger and freight steamboat service, once important throughout Chesapeake Bay and its many tributary rivers, ended in 1962.[[45]](#cite_note-47)

Baltimore was a major war production center during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). The biggest operations were [Bethlehem Steel](/wiki/Bethlehem_Steel "Bethlehem Steel")'s Fairfield Yard, which built [Liberty ships](/wiki/Liberty_ship "Liberty ship"); and [Glenn Martin](/wiki/Glenn_L._Martin_Company "Glenn L. Martin Company"), an aircraft manufacturer.

Maryland experienced population growth following World War II. Beginning in the 1960s, as suburban growth took hold around Washington, D.C. and Baltimore, the state began to take on a more mid-Atlantic culture as opposed to the traditionally Southern and Tidewater culture that previously dominated most of the state. Agricultural tracts gave way to residential communities, some of them carefully planned such as [Columbia](/wiki/Columbia,_Maryland "Columbia, Maryland"), [St. Charles](/wiki/St._Charles,_Maryland "St. Charles, Maryland"), and [Montgomery Village](/wiki/Montgomery_Village,_Maryland "Montgomery Village, Maryland"). Concurrently the [Interstate Highway System](/wiki/Interstate_Highway_System "Interstate Highway System") was built throughout the state, most notably [I-95](/wiki/Interstate_95_in_Maryland "Interstate 95 in Maryland"), [I-695](/wiki/Interstate_695_(Maryland) "Interstate 695 (Maryland)"), and the [Capital Beltway](/wiki/Interstate_495_(Capital_Beltway) "Interstate 495 (Capital Beltway)"), altering travel patterns. In 1952, the eastern and western halves of Maryland were linked for the first time by the [Chesapeake Bay Bridge](/wiki/Chesapeake_Bay_Bridge "Chesapeake Bay Bridge"), which replaced a nearby [ferry](/wiki/Ferry "Ferry") service.[[46]](#cite_note-baybridge.com-history-48)

Maryland's regions experienced economic changes following WWII. Heavy manufacturing declined in Baltimore. In Maryland's four westernmost counties, industrial, railroad, and coal mining jobs declined. On the lower [Eastern Shore](/wiki/Eastern_Shore_of_Maryland "Eastern Shore of Maryland"), family farms were bought up by major concerns and large-scale poultry farms and vegetable farming became prevalent. In Southern Maryland, tobacco farming nearly vanished due to suburban development and a state tobacco buy-out program in the 1990s.

In an effort to reverse depopulation due to the loss of working-class industries, Baltimore initiated [urban renewal](/wiki/Urban_renewal "Urban renewal") projects in the 1960s with [Charles Center](/wiki/Charles_Center "Charles Center") and the [Baltimore World Trade Center](/wiki/Baltimore_World_Trade_Center "Baltimore World Trade Center"). Some resulted in the break-up of intact residential neighborhoods, producing social volatility, and some older residential areas around the harbor have had units renovated and have become popular with new populations.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Geography")]

See also: [Geography of Maryland](/wiki/Geography_of_Maryland "Geography of Maryland"), [List of islands of Maryland](/wiki/List_of_islands_of_Maryland "List of islands of Maryland"), and [List of rivers of Maryland](/wiki/List_of_rivers_of_Maryland "List of rivers of Maryland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/1/1e/MDGeoReg.PNG/250px-MDGeoReg.PNG)](/wiki/File:MDGeoReg.PNG)

Physical regions of Maryland

Maryland has an area of 12,406.68 square miles (32,133.2 km2) and is comparable in overall area with [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium").[[47]](#cite_note-49) It is the 42nd-largest and 9th-smallest state and is closest in size to the state of [Hawaii](/wiki/Hawaii "Hawaii") that, with its 10,930.98 square miles (28,311.1 km2), is the next smallest state. The next largest state is Maryland's neighbor, [West Virginia](/wiki/West_Virginia "West Virginia"), which is nearly twice the size of Maryland with its area of 24,229.76 square miles (62,754.8 km2).

### Description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Description")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Deep_Creek_Lake_Maryland_Panoramic_View.jpg/250px-Deep_Creek_Lake_Maryland_Panoramic_View.jpg)](/wiki/File:Deep_Creek_Lake_Maryland_Panoramic_View.jpg)

[Western Maryland](/wiki/Western_Maryland "Western Maryland") is known for its heavily forested mountains. A panoramic view of [Deep Creek Lake](/wiki/Deep_Creek_Lake "Deep Creek Lake") and the surrounding [Appalachian Mountains](/wiki/Appalachian_Mountains "Appalachian Mountains") in [Garrett County](/wiki/Garrett_County,_Maryland "Garrett County, Maryland").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Great_Falls%2C_Maryland%2C_cliffs.jpg/250px-Great_Falls%2C_Maryland%2C_cliffs.jpg)](/wiki/File:Great_Falls,_Maryland,_cliffs.jpg)

[Great Falls](/wiki/Great_Falls_(Potomac_River) "Great Falls (Potomac River)") on the [Potomac River](/wiki/Potomac_River "Potomac River")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Patapsco_fg01.jpg/250px-Patapsco_fg01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Patapsco_fg01.jpg)

[Patapsco River](/wiki/Patapsco_River "Patapsco River") includes the [Thomas Viaduct](/wiki/Thomas_Viaduct "Thomas Viaduct") and is part of the [Patapsco Valley State Park](/wiki/Patapsco_Valley_State_Park "Patapsco Valley State Park"); the river forms [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore")'s [Inner Harbor](/wiki/Inner_Harbor "Inner Harbor") as it empties into the [Chesapeake Bay](/wiki/Chesapeake_Bay "Chesapeake Bay").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Sunset_over_the_marsh_at_Cardinal_Cove_-_NOAA.jpg/250px-Sunset_over_the_marsh_at_Cardinal_Cove_-_NOAA.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sunset_over_the_marsh_at_Cardinal_Cove_-_NOAA.jpg)

Sunset over a marsh at Cardinal Cove on the [Patuxent River](/wiki/Patuxent_River "Patuxent River")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/ChesapeakeTidalWetlands.jpg/250px-ChesapeakeTidalWetlands.jpg)](/wiki/File:ChesapeakeTidalWetlands.jpg)

Tidal wetlands of the [Chesapeake Bay](/wiki/Chesapeake_Bay "Chesapeake Bay"), the largest [estuary](/wiki/Estuary "Estuary") in the nation and the largest water feature in Maryland

Maryland possesses a variety of [topography](/wiki/Topography "Topography") within its borders, contributing to its nickname *America in Miniature*.[[48]](#cite_note-50) It ranges from sandy [dunes](/wiki/Dunes "Dunes") dotted with seagrass in the east, to low marshlands teeming with wildlife and large [bald cypress](/wiki/Taxodium "Taxodium") near the Chesapeake Bay, to gently rolling hills of oak forests in the [Piedmont Region](/wiki/Piedmont_(United_States) "Piedmont (United States)"), and [pine](/wiki/Pine "Pine") groves in the [Maryland mountains](/wiki/List_of_mountains_in_Maryland "List of mountains in Maryland") to the west.

Maryland is bounded on its north by [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"), on its north and east by [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware "Delaware"), on its east by the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean"), and on its south and west, across the [Potomac River](/wiki/Potomac_River "Potomac River"), by [West Virginia](/wiki/West_Virginia "West Virginia") and [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia"). The mid-portion of its border with Virginia is interrupted by [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), which sits on land that was originally part of [Montgomery](/wiki/Montgomery_County,_Maryland "Montgomery County, Maryland") and [Prince George's](/wiki/Prince_George%27s_County,_Maryland "Prince George's County, Maryland") counties and including [Georgetown, Maryland](/wiki/Georgetown_(Washington,_D.C.) "Georgetown (Washington, D.C.)"), which was ceded to the United States federal government in 1790 to form the Washington, D.C. [Chesapeake Bay](/wiki/Chesapeake_Bay "Chesapeake Bay") nearly bisects the state, and the counties east of the bay are known collectively as the [Eastern Shore](/wiki/Eastern_Shore_of_Maryland "Eastern Shore of Maryland").

Most of the state's waterways are part of the Chesapeake Bay watershed, with the exceptions of a tiny portion of extreme western [Garrett County](/wiki/Garrett_County,_Maryland "Garrett County, Maryland") (drained by the [Youghiogheny River](/wiki/Youghiogheny_River "Youghiogheny River") as part of the watershed of the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River")), the eastern half of Worcester County (which drains into Maryland's Atlantic coastal bays), and a small portion of the state's northeast corner (which drains into the [Delaware River](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River") watershed). So prominent is the Chesapeake in Maryland's geography and economic life that there has been periodic agitation to change the state's official nickname to the "Bay State", a nickname that has been used by [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts") for decades.

The highest point in Maryland, with an elevation of 3,360 feet (1,020 m), is [Hoye Crest on Backbone Mountain](/wiki/Backbone_Mountain "Backbone Mountain"), in the southwest corner of [Garrett County](/wiki/Garrett_County,_Maryland "Garrett County, Maryland"), near the border with West Virginia, and near the headwaters of the North Branch of the Potomac River. Close to the small town of [Hancock](/wiki/Hancock,_Maryland "Hancock, Maryland"), in western Maryland, about two-thirds of the way across the state, less than 2 miles (3.2 km) separates its borders,[[49]](#cite_note-51) the [Mason–Dixon line](/wiki/Mason%E2%80%93Dixon_line "Mason–Dixon line") to the north, and the northwards-arching [Potomac River](/wiki/Potomac_River "Potomac River") to the south.

Portions of Maryland are included in various official and unofficial geographic regions. For example, the [Delmarva Peninsula](/wiki/Delmarva_Peninsula "Delmarva Peninsula") is composed of the Eastern Shore counties of Maryland, the entire [state of Delaware](/wiki/Delaware "Delaware"), and the two counties that make up the [Eastern Shore of Virginia](/wiki/Eastern_Shore_of_Virginia "Eastern Shore of Virginia"), whereas the westernmost counties of Maryland are considered part of [Appalachia](/wiki/Appalachia "Appalachia"). Much of the Baltimore–Washington corridor lies just south of the Piedmont in the Coastal Plain,[[50]](#cite_note-52) though it straddles the border between the two regions.

### Geology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Geology")]

Earthquakes in Maryland are infrequent and small due to the state's distance from seismic/earthquake zones.[[51]](#cite_note-53)[[52]](#cite_note-54) The [M](/wiki/Moment_magnitude_scale "Moment magnitude scale")5.8 [Virginia earthquake in 2011](/wiki/2011_Virginia_earthquake "2011 Virginia earthquake") was felt moderately throughout Maryland. Buildings in the state are not well-designed for earthquakes and can suffer damage easily.[[53]](#cite_note-55) As well as this, notably [a M4.8 earthquake](/wiki/2024_New_Jersey_earthquake "2024 New Jersey earthquake") from [Tewksbury](/wiki/Tewksbury_Township,_New_Jersey "Tewksbury Township, New Jersey") in central New Jersey was felt slightly throughout Maryland.[[54]](#cite_note-56)

Maryland has no natural lakes, mostly due to the lack of glacial history in the area.[[55]](#cite_note-57) All lakes in the state today were constructed, mostly via dams.[[56]](#cite_note-58) [Buckel's Bog](/wiki/Buckel%27s_Bog "Buckel's Bog") is believed by geologists to have been a remnant of a former natural lake.[[57]](#cite_note-59)

Maryland has shale formations containing natural gas, where fracking is theoretically possible.[[58]](#cite_note-natgas-60)

### Flora

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Flora")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/BESusan.JPG/250px-BESusan.JPG)](/wiki/File:BESusan.JPG)

[Black-eyed susans](/wiki/Rudbeckia_hirta "Rudbeckia hirta"), the state flower, grow throughout much of the state.[[59]](#cite_note-maag-flower-61)

As is typical of states on the [East Coast](/wiki/East_Coast_of_the_United_States "East Coast of the United States"), Maryland's plant life is abundant and healthy. An adequate volume of annual precipitation helps to support many types of plants, including [seagrass](/wiki/Seagrass "Seagrass") and various [reeds](/wiki/Phragmites "Phragmites") at the smaller end of the spectrum to the gigantic [Wye Oak](/wiki/Wye_Oak "Wye Oak"), a huge example of [white oak](/wiki/White_oak "White oak"), the state tree, which can grow over 70 feet (21 m) tall.

[Middle Atlantic coastal forests](/wiki/Middle_Atlantic_coastal_forests "Middle Atlantic coastal forests"), typical of the southeastern [Atlantic coastal plain](/wiki/Atlantic_coastal_plain "Atlantic coastal plain"), grow around Chesapeake Bay and on the [Delmarva Peninsula](/wiki/Delmarva_Peninsula "Delmarva Peninsula"). Moving west, a mixture of [Northeastern coastal forests](/wiki/Northeastern_coastal_forests "Northeastern coastal forests") and [Southeastern mixed forests](/wiki/Southeastern_mixed_forests "Southeastern mixed forests") cover the central part of the state. The [Appalachian Mountains](/wiki/Appalachian_Mountains "Appalachian Mountains") of western Maryland are home to [Appalachian-Blue Ridge forests](/wiki/Appalachian-Blue_Ridge_forests "Appalachian-Blue Ridge forests"). These give way to [Appalachian mixed mesophytic forests](/wiki/Appalachian_mixed_mesophytic_forests "Appalachian mixed mesophytic forests") near the West Virginia border.[[60]](#cite_note-ecoregions-62)

Many foreign species are cultivated in the state, some as ornamentals, others as novelty species. Included among these are the [crape myrtle](/wiki/Lagerstroemia "Lagerstroemia"), [Italian cypress](/wiki/Italian_cypress "Italian cypress"), [southern magnolia](/wiki/Magnolia_grandiflora "Magnolia grandiflora"), [live oak](/wiki/Live_oak "Live oak") in the warmer parts of the state,[[61]](#cite_note-63) and even [hardy palm trees](/wiki/Hardy_palms "Hardy palms") in the warmer central and eastern parts of the state.[[62]](#cite_note-64) USDA plant [hardiness zones](/wiki/Hardiness_zone "Hardiness zone") in the state range from Zones 5 and 6 in the extreme western part of the state to Zone 7 in the central part, and Zone 8 around the southern part of the coast, the bay area, and parts of [metropolitan Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore_Metropolitan_Area "Baltimore Metropolitan Area").[[63]](#cite_note-Arbor_Day_Foundation-65) Invasive plant species, such as [kudzu](/wiki/Kudzu "Kudzu"), [tree of heaven](/wiki/Tree_of_heaven "Tree of heaven"), [multiflora rose](/wiki/Multiflora_rose "Multiflora rose"), and [Japanese stiltgrass](/wiki/Microstegium_vimineum "Microstegium vimineum"), stifle growth of endemic plant life.[[64]](#cite_note-66) Maryland's state flower, the [black-eyed susan](/wiki/Rudbeckia_hirta "Rudbeckia hirta"), grows in abundance in wild flower groups throughout the state.

### Fauna

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Fauna")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Assateague_Island_horses_August_2009_4.jpg/250px-Assateague_Island_horses_August_2009_4.jpg)](/wiki/File:Assateague_Island_horses_August_2009_4.jpg)

A feral [Chincoteague Pony](/wiki/Chincoteague_Pony "Chincoteague Pony") on Assateague Island on Maryland's Atlantic coastal islands

The state harbors a considerable number of [white-tailed deer](/wiki/White-tailed_deer "White-tailed deer"), especially in the woody and mountainous west of the state, and overpopulation can become a problem. Mammals can be found ranging from the mountains in the west to the central areas and include [black bears](/wiki/American_black_bear "American black bear"),[[65]](#cite_note-Maryland_Wildlife-67) [bobcats](/wiki/Bobcat "Bobcat"),[[66]](#cite_note-68) foxes, [coyotes](/wiki/Coyote "Coyote"),[[67]](#cite_note-69) raccoons, and otters.[[65]](#cite_note-Maryland_Wildlife-67)

There is a population of rare wild (feral) horses found on [Assateague Island](/wiki/Assateague_Island "Assateague Island").[[68]](#cite_note-horse-70) They are believed to be descended from horses that escaped from Spanish galleon shipwrecks.[[68]](#cite_note-horse-70) Every year during the last week of July, they are captured and swim across a shallow bay for sale at [Chincoteague, Virginia](/wiki/Chincoteague,_Virginia "Chincoteague, Virginia"), a conservation technique which ensures the tiny island is not overrun by the horses.[[68]](#cite_note-horse-70) The ponies and their sale were popularized by the children's book, *[Misty of Chincoteague](/wiki/Misty_of_Chincoteague_(novel) "Misty of Chincoteague (novel)").*

The purebred [Chesapeake Bay Retriever](/wiki/Chesapeake_Bay_Retriever "Chesapeake Bay Retriever") dog was bred specifically for water sports, hunting and search and rescue in the Chesapeake area.[[69]](#cite_note-Maryland1-71) In 1878, the Chesapeake Bay Retriever was the first individual retriever breed recognized by the [American Kennel Club](/wiki/American_Kennel_Club "American Kennel Club").[[69]](#cite_note-Maryland1-71) and was later adopted by the [University of Maryland, Baltimore County](/wiki/University_of_Maryland,_Baltimore_County "University of Maryland, Baltimore County") as their mascot.

Maryland's reptile and amphibian population includes the [diamondback terrapin](/wiki/Diamondback_terrapin "Diamondback terrapin") turtle, which was adopted as the mascot of [University of Maryland, College Park](/wiki/University_of_Maryland,_College_Park "University of Maryland, College Park"), as well as the threatened [Eastern box turtle](/wiki/Eastern_box_turtle "Eastern box turtle").[[70]](#cite_note-72) The state is part of the territory of the [Baltimore oriole](/wiki/Baltimore_oriole "Baltimore oriole"), which is the official state bird and mascot of the [MLB](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") team the [Baltimore Orioles](/wiki/Baltimore_Orioles "Baltimore Orioles").[[71]](#cite_note-73) Aside from the oriole, [435 other species of birds](/wiki/List_of_Maryland_birds "List of Maryland birds") have been reported from Maryland.[[72]](#cite_note-MRC2-74)

The state insect is the [Baltimore checkerspot butterfly](/wiki/Baltimore_checkerspot "Baltimore checkerspot"), although it is not as common in Maryland as it is in the southern edge of its range.[[73]](#cite_note-75)

### Environment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Environment")]

Maryland joined with neighboring states during the end of the 20th century to improve the health of the [Chesapeake Bay](/wiki/Chesapeake_Bay "Chesapeake Bay"). The bay's aquatic life and seafood industry have been threatened by development and by fertilizer and livestock waste entering the bay.[[74]](#cite_note-76)[[75]](#cite_note-hog-77)

In 2007, Forbes.com rated Maryland as the fifth "Greenest" state in the country, behind three of the [Pacific States](/wiki/Pacific_States "Pacific States") and Vermont. Maryland ranks 40th in total energy consumption nationwide, and it managed less toxic waste per capita than all but six states in 2005.[[76]](#cite_note-78) In April 2007, Maryland joined the [Regional Greenhouse Gas Initiative](/wiki/Regional_Greenhouse_Gas_Initiative "Regional Greenhouse Gas Initiative") (RGGI) — a regional initiative, formed by all the Northeastern states, Washington, D.C., and three Canadian provinces, to reduce greenhouse gas emissions.[[77]](#cite_note-79) In March 2017, Maryland became the first state with proven gas reserves to ban fracking by passing a law against it. Vermont has such a law, but no shale gas, and New York has such a ban, though it was made by executive order.[[58]](#cite_note-natgas-60)

In 2023, [AES Corporation](/wiki/AES_Corporation "AES Corporation") announced its intent to retire the 23-year-old [Warrior Run coal plant](/wiki/Warrior_Run_Generating_Station "Warrior Run Generating Station") in June 2024. It was the state's last coal-fired power plant that did not already have plans to shut down.[[78]](#cite_note-80)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Climate")]

Further information: [Climate change in Maryland](/wiki/Climate_change_in_Maryland "Climate change in Maryland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/K%C3%B6ppen_Climate_Types_Maryland.png/250px-K%C3%B6ppen_Climate_Types_Maryland.png)](/wiki/File:K%C3%B6ppen_Climate_Types_Maryland.png)

[Köppen climate types](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") of Maryland, using 1991–2020 [climate normals](/wiki/Climatological_normal "Climatological normal")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Baltimore_Snowpocalypse.jpg/250px-Baltimore_Snowpocalypse.jpg)](/wiki/File:Baltimore_Snowpocalypse.jpg)

Winter on Lancaster Street in [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore")'s [Fells Point](/wiki/Fell%27s_Point,_Baltimore "Fell's Point, Baltimore")

Maryland has a wide array of climates, due to local variances in elevation, proximity to water, and protection from colder weather due to [downslope winds](/wiki/Katabatic_wind "Katabatic wind").
The eastern half of Maryland, which includes [Ocean City](/wiki/Ocean_City,_Maryland "Ocean City, Maryland"), [Salisbury](/wiki/Salisbury,_Maryland "Salisbury, Maryland"), [Annapolis](/wiki/Annapolis "Annapolis"), and the southern and eastern suburbs of Washington, D.C., and [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore"), lies on the [Atlantic Coastal Plain](/wiki/Atlantic_Coastal_Plain "Atlantic Coastal Plain"), with flat topography and sandy or muddy soil. This region has a [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") *Cfa*), with hot, humid summers and cool to cold winters; it falls under USDA [Hardiness zone](/wiki/Hardiness_zone "Hardiness zone") 8a.[[63]](#cite_note-Arbor_Day_Foundation-65)

The [Piedmont](/wiki/Piedmont_(United_States) "Piedmont (United States)") region, which includes northern and western greater Baltimore, [Westminster](/wiki/Westminster,_Maryland "Westminster, Maryland"), [Gaithersburg](/wiki/Gaithersburg,_Maryland "Gaithersburg, Maryland"), [Frederick](/wiki/Frederick,_Maryland "Frederick, Maryland"), and [Hagerstown](/wiki/Hagerstown,_Maryland "Hagerstown, Maryland"), has average seasonal snowfall totals generally exceeding 20 inches (51 cm), and, as part of USDA Hardiness zones 7b and 7a,[[63]](#cite_note-Arbor_Day_Foundation-65) temperatures below 10 °F (−12 °C) are less rare. From the [Cumberland Valley](/wiki/Cumberland_Valley "Cumberland Valley") on westward, the climate begins to transition to a [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") (Köppen *Dfa*).

In Western Maryland, the higher elevations of [Allegany](/wiki/Allegany_County,_Maryland "Allegany County, Maryland") and [Garrett](/wiki/Garrett_County,_Maryland "Garrett County, Maryland") counties, including the cities of [Cumberland](/wiki/Cumberland,_Maryland "Cumberland, Maryland"), [Frostburg](/wiki/Frostburg,_Maryland "Frostburg, Maryland"), and [Oakland](/wiki/Oakland,_Maryland "Oakland, Maryland"), display more characteristics of the humid continental zone, due in part to elevation. They fall under USDA Hardiness zones 6b and below.[[63]](#cite_note-Arbor_Day_Foundation-65)

Precipitation in the state is characteristic of the [East Coast](/wiki/East_Coast_of_the_United_States "East Coast of the United States"). Annual rainfall ranges from 35 to 45 inches (890 to 1,140 mm) with more in higher elevations. Nearly every part of Maryland receives 3.5–4.5 inches (89–114 mm) per month of rain. Average annual snowfall varies from 9 inches (23 cm) in the coastal areas to over 100 inches (250 cm) in the western mountains of the state.[[79]](#cite_note-81)

Because of its location near the [Atlantic Coast](/wiki/East_Coast_of_the_United_States "East Coast of the United States"), Maryland is somewhat vulnerable to [tropical cyclones](/wiki/Tropical_cyclones "Tropical cyclones"), although the [Delmarva Peninsula](/wiki/Delmarva_Peninsula "Delmarva Peninsula") and the outer banks of [North Carolina](/wiki/North_Carolina "North Carolina") provide a large buffer, such that strikes from major hurricanes (category 3 or above) occur infrequently. More often, Maryland gets the remnants of a tropical system that has already come ashore and released most of its energy. Maryland averages around 30–40 days of thunderstorms a year, and averages around six tornado strikes annually.[[80]](#cite_note-Annual_average_number_of_tornadoes-82)

### Cities and metro areas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Cities and metro areas")]

See also: [Maryland statistical areas](/wiki/Maryland_statistical_areas "Maryland statistical areas")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Maryland_population_density_2020.png/250px-Maryland_population_density_2020.png)](/wiki/File:Maryland_population_density_2020.png)

Maryland population distribution map; Maryland's population is concentrated mostly in the [Washington–Baltimore combined statistical area](/wiki/Washington%E2%80%93Baltimore_combined_statistical_area "Washington–Baltimore combined statistical area").

Most of the population of Maryland lives in the central region of the state, in the [Baltimore metropolitan area](/wiki/Baltimore_metropolitan_area "Baltimore metropolitan area") and [Washington metropolitan area](/wiki/Washington_metropolitan_area "Washington metropolitan area"), both of which are part of the [Baltimore–Washington metropolitan area](/wiki/Baltimore%E2%80%93Washington_metropolitan_area "Baltimore–Washington metropolitan area"). The majority of Maryland's population is concentrated in the cities and suburbs surrounding [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), and in and around [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore"), Maryland's most populous city. Historically, these and many other Maryland cities developed along the [Fall Line](/wiki/Atlantic_Seaboard_fall_line "Atlantic Seaboard fall line"), the line along which rivers, brooks, and streams are interrupted by rapids and waterfalls. Maryland's capital city, [Annapolis](/wiki/Annapolis,_Maryland "Annapolis, Maryland"), is one exception to this pattern since it lies along the banks of the [Severn River](/wiki/Severn_River_(Maryland) "Severn River (Maryland)"), close to where it empties into the [Chesapeake Bay](/wiki/Chesapeake_Bay "Chesapeake Bay").

The Eastern Shore is less populous and more rural, as are the counties of western Maryland. The two westernmost counties of Maryland, [Allegany](/wiki/Allegany_County,_Maryland "Allegany County, Maryland") and [Garrett](/wiki/Garrett_County,_Maryland "Garrett County, Maryland"), are mountainous and sparsely populated, resembling West Virginia and [Appalachia](/wiki/Appalachia "Appalachia") more than they do the rest of the state. Both eastern and western Maryland are, however, dotted with cities of regional importance, such as [Ocean City](/wiki/Ocean_City,_Maryland "Ocean City, Maryland"), [Princess Anne](/wiki/Princess_Anne,_Maryland "Princess Anne, Maryland"), and [Salisbury](/wiki/Salisbury,_Maryland "Salisbury, Maryland") on the [Eastern Shore](/wiki/Eastern_Shore_of_Maryland "Eastern Shore of Maryland") and [Cumberland](/wiki/Cumberland,_Maryland "Cumberland, Maryland"), [Frostburg](/wiki/Frostburg,_Maryland "Frostburg, Maryland"), and [Hancock](/wiki/Hancock,_Maryland "Hancock, Maryland") in [Western Maryland](/wiki/Western_Maryland "Western Maryland"). Southern Maryland is still somewhat rural, but suburbanization from Washington, D.C., has encroached significantly since the 1960s; important local population centers include [Lexington Park](/wiki/Lexington_Park,_Maryland "Lexington Park, Maryland"), [Prince Frederick](/wiki/Prince_Frederick,_Maryland "Prince Frederick, Maryland"), [California](/wiki/California,_Maryland "California, Maryland"), and [Waldorf](/wiki/Waldorf,_Maryland "Waldorf, Maryland").[[91]](#cite_note-93)[[92]](#cite_note-94)

In the [2020 United States census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau") found that population of Maryland was 6,185,278 people, a 7.1% increase from the [2010 United States census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census").[[93]](#cite_note-censusdata-95) The United States Census Bureau estimated that the population of Maryland was 6,045,680 on July 1, 2019, a 4.71% increase from the 2010 United States census and an increase of 2,962, from the prior year. This includes a natural increase since the last census of 269,166 (464,251 births minus 275,093 deaths) and an increase due to net migration of 116,713 people into the state. [Immigration](/wiki/Immigration_to_the_United_States "Immigration to the United States") from outside the United States resulted in a net increase of 129,730 people, and migration within the country produced a net loss of 13,017 people.[[94]](#cite_note-PopEstUS-96) In 2018, The top countries of origin for Maryland's immigrants were [El Salvador](/wiki/El_Salvador "El Salvador") (11%), [India](/wiki/India "India") (6%), [China](/wiki/China "China") (5%), [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria "Nigeria") (5%), and the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines") (4%).[[95]](#cite_note-97) The [center of population](/wiki/Center_of_population "Center of population") of Maryland is located on the county line between [Anne Arundel County](/wiki/Anne_Arundel_County,_Maryland "Anne Arundel County, Maryland") and [Howard County](/wiki/Howard_County,_Maryland "Howard County, Maryland"), in the [unincorporated community](/wiki/Unincorporated_area "Unincorporated area") of [Jessup](/wiki/Jessup,_Maryland "Jessup, Maryland").[[96]](#cite_note-98)

Maryland's history as a [border state](/wiki/Border_states_(Civil_War) "Border states (Civil War)") has led it to exhibit characteristics of both the [Northern](/wiki/Northern_United_States "Northern United States") and the [Southern](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States") regions of the United States. Generally, rural [Western Maryland](/wiki/Western_Maryland "Western Maryland") between the [West Virginian Panhandle](/wiki/Eastern_Panhandle_of_West_Virginia "Eastern Panhandle of West Virginia") and [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania") has an [Appalachian](/wiki/Appalachia "Appalachia") culture; the [Southern](/wiki/Southern_Maryland "Southern Maryland") and Eastern Shore regions of Maryland embody a [Southern culture](/wiki/Culture_of_the_Southern_United_States "Culture of the Southern United States"),[[97]](#cite_note-99) while densely populated Central Maryland – radiating outward from [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore") and [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.") – has more in common with that of the [Northeast](/wiki/Northeastern_United_States "Northeastern United States").[[98]](#cite_note-100) The U.S. Census Bureau designates Maryland as one of the [South Atlantic States](/wiki/South_Atlantic_States "South Atlantic States"),[[99]](#cite_note-101)[[100]](#cite_note-LibGds-102) but it is commonly associated with the [Mid-Atlantic States](/wiki/Mid-Atlantic_States "Mid-Atlantic States") by other federal agencies, the media, and some residents.[[101]](#cite_note-LOC-103)[[102]](#cite_note-EPA-104)

According to [HUD](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Housing_and_Urban_Development "United States Department of Housing and Urban Development")'s 2022 [Annual Homeless Assessment Report](/wiki/Annual_Homeless_Assessment_Report_to_Congress "Annual Homeless Assessment Report to Congress"), there were an estimated 5,349 [homeless](/wiki/Homelessness "Homelessness") people in Maryland.[[103]](#cite_note-105)[[104]](#cite_note-106)

As of 2016, the most spoken languages in Maryland other than English were [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language") (9%), [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language") (1.2%), West African languages (mostly [Yoruba](/wiki/Yoruba_language "Yoruba language") and [Igbo](/wiki/Igbo_language "Igbo language"), 1%), [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") (1%), [Korean](/wiki/Korean_language "Korean language") (0.7%), [Afro-Asiatic languages](/wiki/Afroasiatic_languages "Afroasiatic languages") (mostly [Amharic](/wiki/Amharic "Amharic"), 0.6% and [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic"), 0.4%), and [Tagalog](/wiki/Tagalog_language "Tagalog language") (0.6%). Other languages with a large number of speakers in Maryland include [Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_language "Vietnamese language") (0.4%), [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language") (0.4%), [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi") (0.3%), [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu") (0.3%), [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language") (0.3%), [Nepali](/wiki/Nepali_language "Nepali language") (0.3%), [Haitian Creole](/wiki/Haitian_Creole "Haitian Creole") (0.2%), and [Telugu](/wiki/Telugu_language "Telugu language") (0.2%).[[120]](#cite_note-123)[[121]](#cite_note-124)
