In 2019, [non-Hispanic white Americans](/wiki/Non-Hispanic_whites "Non-Hispanic whites") were 49.8% of Maryland's population ([White Americans](/wiki/White_Americans_in_Maryland "White Americans in Maryland"), including [White Hispanics](/wiki/White_Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "White Hispanic and Latino Americans"), were 57.3%), which made Maryland a [majority minority](/wiki/Majority_minority "Majority minority") state.[[119]](#cite_note-ACSDT1Y2019-122) 50.2% of Maryland's population is non-white, or is Hispanic or Latino, the highest percentage of any state on the [East Coast](/wiki/East_Coast_of_the_United_States "East Coast of the United States"), and the highest percentage after the majority-minority states of [Hawaii](/wiki/Hawaii "Hawaii"), [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico"), [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas"), [California](/wiki/California "California"), [Nevada](/wiki/Nevada "Nevada"), and [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.")[[129]](#cite_note-132) By 2031, minorities are projected to become the majority of voting eligible residents of Maryland.[[130]](#cite_note-133) Maryland's multiculturalism and diversity can be explained by its historically large African American population, and immigration brought by the importance of the D.C. and Baltimore areas, especially from Central America, Africa, the Caribbean, and Asia.

[African Americans](/wiki/African_Americans_in_Maryland "African Americans in Maryland") form a sizable portion of the state's population, 31.1% as of 2020.[[125]](#cite_note-census.gov-128) Most are descendants of people transported to the area as slaves from West Africa. Concentrations of African Americans live in [Baltimore City](/wiki/Baltimore,_Maryland "Baltimore, Maryland"), [Prince George's County](/wiki/Prince_George%27s_County,_Maryland "Prince George's County, Maryland"), [Charles County](/wiki/Charles_County,_Maryland "Charles County, Maryland"), western parts of [Baltimore County](/wiki/Baltimore_County,_Maryland "Baltimore County, Maryland"), and the southern Eastern Shore. Charles County and Prince George's County are the two counties where African Americans are the most successful monetarily in the country, with average household incomes much higher than in the rest of the country. As a former slave state, Maryland has had a large African-American population for much of its history; African American populations have increased over time with the [Great Migration](/wiki/Great_Migration_(African_American) "Great Migration (African American)") to the D.C. and Baltimore areas, and in more recent times with the [New Great Migration](/wiki/New_Great_Migration "New Great Migration") and with movement out from Washington D.C. into Montgomery, Prince George's, and Charles counties, as a result of gentrification and rising housing costs in D.C. causing many African Americans to leave. Prince George's County in particular has been a magnet for African Americans from D.C. to move to for decades; it is often referred to as "Ward 9" of D.C.

Maryland has by far the highest percentage of residents born in Africa out of any state; residents of African descent include 20th-century and later immigrants from [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigerian_American "Nigerian American"), particularly of the [Igbo](/wiki/Igbo_American "Igbo American") and [Yoruba](/wiki/Yoruba_people "Yoruba people") tribes; [Ethiopia](/wiki/Ethiopia "Ethiopia"), particularly [Amharas](/wiki/Amhara_people "Amhara people") with significant [Oromo](/wiki/Oromo_people "Oromo people") and [Tigrayan](/wiki/Tigrayans "Tigrayans") populations; [Cameroon](/wiki/Cameroon "Cameroon"), [Ghana](/wiki/Ghana "Ghana"), [Sierra Leone](/wiki/Sierra_Leone "Sierra Leone"), and [Kenya](/wiki/Kenya "Kenya").[[131]](#cite_note-134) Maryland also hosts populations from other African and [Caribbean](/wiki/West_Indian_American "West Indian American") nations. Maryland's African immigrant population is generally well-educated and is most concentrated in the inner suburbs of Baltimore and D.C. Nigerians are the fourth-largest immigrant group in Maryland, and are largely concentrated in the Baltimore area and surrounding suburbs, as well as Prince George's county. Many immigrants from the [Horn of Africa](/wiki/Horn_of_Africa "Horn of Africa"), especially [Ethiopia](/wiki/Ethiopia "Ethiopia"), have settled in Maryland, with large communities in the suburbs of Washington, D.C., particularly in Montgomery and Prince George's counties. The [Washington metropolitan area](/wiki/Washington_metropolitan_area "Washington metropolitan area") has the world's largest population of [Ethiopians](/wiki/Ethiopians_in_Washington,_D.C. "Ethiopians in Washington, D.C.") outside of Ethiopia.[[132]](#cite_note-Westley-135) The Ethiopian community of Greater D.C. was historically based in the [Adams Morgan](/wiki/Adams_Morgan "Adams Morgan") and [Shaw](/wiki/Shaw,_Washington,_D.C. "Shaw, Washington, D.C.") neighborhoods of Washington, D.C., but as the community has grown, many Ethiopians have settled in [Silver Spring](/wiki/Silver_Spring,_Maryland "Silver Spring, Maryland").[[133]](#cite_note-136) The Ethiopian American population in Maryland and the rest of the D.C. area is largely [Amharic](/wiki/Amharic "Amharic")-speaking, but there are significant numbers of speakers of [Oromo](/wiki/Oromo_language "Oromo language") and [Tigrinya](/wiki/Tigrinya_language "Tigrinya language") speakers as well. The Washington metropolitan area is also home to a large [Eritrean](/wiki/Eritrean_Americans "Eritrean Americans") community.

The top reported ancestries by Maryland residents are: [German](/wiki/German_American "German American") (15%), [Irish](/wiki/Irish_American "Irish American") (11%), [English](/wiki/English_American "English American") (8%), [American](/wiki/Americans "Americans") (7%), [Italian](/wiki/Italian_American "Italian American") (6%), and [Polish](/wiki/Polish_American "Polish American") (3%).[[134]](#cite_note-137)

[Irish American](/wiki/Irish_American "Irish American") populations can be found [throughout the Baltimore area](/wiki/History_of_the_Irish_in_Baltimore "History of the Irish in Baltimore"),[[135]](#cite_note-teaching.msa.maryland.gov-138) and the Northern and Eastern suburbs of Washington, D.C., in Maryland, who were descendants of those who moved out to the suburbs[[136]](#cite_note-abcswamp-139) of Washington, D.C.'s once predominantly Irish neighborhoods[[136]](#cite_note-abcswamp-139)[[137]](#cite_note-:4-140)), as well as Western Maryland, where Irish immigrant laborers helped to build the B&O Railroad.[[135]](#cite_note-teaching.msa.maryland.gov-138) Smaller but much older Irish populations can be found in Southern Maryland, with some roots dating as far back as the early Maryland colony.[[138]](#cite_note-141) This population, however, still remains culturally very active and yearly festivals are held.[[139]](#cite_note-142)

More recent European immigrants of the late 19th and early 20th century settled first in Baltimore, attracted to its industrial jobs. These groups were largely of [Jewish](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Baltimore "History of the Jews in Baltimore"), [Italian](/wiki/History_of_the_Italians_in_Baltimore "History of the Italians in Baltimore"), [Greek](/wiki/History_of_the_Greeks_in_Baltimore "History of the Greeks in Baltimore"), [Polish](/wiki/History_of_the_Poles_in_Baltimore "History of the Poles in Baltimore"), [Czech](/wiki/History_of_the_Czechs_in_Baltimore "History of the Czechs in Baltimore"), [Lithuanian](/wiki/History_of_the_Lithuanians_in_Baltimore "History of the Lithuanians in Baltimore"), [Russian](/wiki/History_of_Russians_in_Baltimore "History of Russians in Baltimore") and [Ukrainian](/wiki/History_of_the_Ukrainians_in_Baltimore "History of the Ukrainians in Baltimore") descent. The Greek community includes a number of [Greek Jews](/wiki/Greek_Jews "Greek Jews").[[140]](#cite_note-143)

The shares of European immigrants born in Eastern Europe increased significantly between 1990 and 2010. Following the dissolution of the Soviet Union, Yugoslavia, and Czechoslovakia, many immigrants from Eastern Europe came to the United States—12 percent of whom currently reside in Maryland.[[141]](#cite_note-144)[[142]](#cite_note-145)

Hispanic immigrants of the later 20th century have settled in [Aspen Hill](/wiki/Aspen_Hill,_Maryland "Aspen Hill, Maryland"), [Hyattsville](/wiki/Hyattsville,_Maryland "Hyattsville, Maryland")/[Langley Park](/wiki/Langley_Park,_Maryland "Langley Park, Maryland"), [Glenmont](/wiki/Glenmont,_Maryland "Glenmont, Maryland")/[Wheaton](/wiki/Wheaton,_Maryland "Wheaton, Maryland"), [Bladensburg](/wiki/Bladensburg,_Maryland "Bladensburg, Maryland"), [Riverdale Park](/wiki/Riverdale_Park,_Maryland "Riverdale Park, Maryland"), [Gaithersburg](/wiki/Gaithersburg,_Maryland "Gaithersburg, Maryland"), as well as [Highlandtown](/wiki/Highlandtown,_Baltimore "Highlandtown, Baltimore") and [Greektown](/wiki/Greektown,_Baltimore "Greektown, Baltimore") in East Baltimore. Maryland has the highest percentage of residents of [Central American](/wiki/Central_America "Central America") origin of any state.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Salvadorans](/wiki/Salvadoran_American "Salvadoran American") are the largest Hispanic group in Maryland, and Maryland has the largest percentage of Salvadoran residents of any state.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The D.C. area also has the highest percentage of Salvadorans of any American metro area, who are particularly concentrated in Prince George's and Montgomery counties, and has the second-highest total number of Salvadorans after the [Los Angeles area](/wiki/Greater_Los_Angeles "Greater Los Angeles"). Other Hispanic groups with significant populations in the state include [Mexicans](/wiki/Mexican_American "Mexican American"), [Guatemalans](/wiki/Guatemalans "Guatemalans"), [Hondurans](/wiki/Honduran_American "Honduran American"), [Dominicans](/wiki/People_of_the_Dominican_Republic "People of the Dominican Republic"), [Peruvians](/wiki/Peruvians "Peruvians"), and [Puerto Ricans](/wiki/Puerto_Ricans "Puerto Ricans"), along with growing populations of [Brazilians](/wiki/Brazilians "Brazilians"), [Colombians](/wiki/Colombians "Colombians"), [Ecuadorians](/wiki/Ecuadorians "Ecuadorians"), and [Bolivians](/wiki/Bolivians "Bolivians"). Maryland's Hispanic population is especially concentrated in Montgomery County and Prince George's County, with other large populations in the Baltimore area and [Frederick County](/wiki/Frederick_County,_Maryland "Frederick County, Maryland"). Maryland has one of the most diverse Hispanic populations in the country, with significant populations from various [Caribbean](/wiki/Spanish_Caribbean "Spanish Caribbean") and [Central American](/wiki/Central_America "Central America") nations.[[143]](#cite_note-146)

Caribbean Americans have a significant presence in Maryland, especially [Jamaican Americans](/wiki/Jamaican_Americans "Jamaican Americans"), who make up 0.6% of the population and have had a significant presence and influence in Maryland's politics and culture; Maryland's current governor, [Wes Moore](/wiki/Wes_Moore "Wes Moore"), is the son of a Jamaican immigrant mother. Other Caribbean American nationalities with a large population in Maryland include [Dominicans](/wiki/People_of_the_Dominican_Republic "People of the Dominican Republic"), [Haitians](/wiki/Haitians "Haitians"), [Trinidadians and Tobagonians](/wiki/Trinidadians_and_Tobagonians "Trinidadians and Tobagonians"), and [Guyanese](/wiki/Guyanese_people "Guyanese people"). Caribbean Americans are most concentrated in Prince George's County, the city of Baltimore, Baltimore County, and Montgomery County.[[144]](#cite_note-147)[[145]](#cite_note-148)[[146]](#cite_note-149) Maryland is home to nearly 17,000 Haitians, according to 2023 Census Bureau data. Roughly 4,200 live in [Wicomico County, Maryland](/wiki/Wicomico_County,_Maryland "Wicomico County, Maryland"), which includes [Salisbury](/wiki/Salisbury,_Maryland "Salisbury, Maryland").[[118]](#cite_note-WP250811-120) Trinidadians and Tobagonians are concentrated in Baltimore City, Baltimore County, and Prince George's County.[[147]](#cite_note-150) There are growing Caribbean populations in [Columbia](/wiki/Columbia,_Maryland "Columbia, Maryland") and [Waldorf](/wiki/Waldorf,_Maryland "Waldorf, Maryland").[[148]](#cite_note-151)

Asian Americans are concentrated in the suburban counties surrounding Washington, D.C., and in Baltimore suburbs, especially [Howard County](/wiki/Howard_County,_Maryland "Howard County, Maryland"), with [Chinese American](/wiki/Chinese_Americans "Chinese Americans"), [Korean American](/wiki/Korean_American "Korean American") and [Taiwanese American](/wiki/Taiwanese_American "Taiwanese American") communities in [Rockville](/wiki/Rockville,_Maryland "Rockville, Maryland"), [Gaithersburg](/wiki/Gaithersburg,_Maryland "Gaithersburg, Maryland"), and [Germantown](/wiki/Germantown,_Maryland "Germantown, Maryland"). Chinese in particular form the second largest group of Asian Americans, and are the largest group in Montgomery County.[[149]](#cite_note-152) Maryland also has a large Korean American population, especially in Howard County, where there is a [Koreatown](/wiki/Koreatown "Koreatown") in [Ellicott City](/wiki/Ellicott_City,_Maryland "Ellicott City, Maryland").[[150]](#cite_note-153)[[151]](#cite_note-154) [Filipino Americans](/wiki/Filipino_American "Filipino American"), the largest group of Southeast Asians, form major communities in Montgomery, Prince George's, and Charles counties; other large groups of Southeast Asians include Vietnamese, who are concentrated in Montgomery County, and Burmese, who are concentrated in Frederick, Howard, and Baltimore Counties.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Maryland has a very large and diverse [South Asian American](/wiki/South_Asian_Americans "South Asian Americans") population that has had a major presence in the state since the 1970s. [Indian Americans](/wiki/Indian_Americans "Indian Americans") are the largest Asian group in Maryland, making up 1.7 percent of the population, and live throughout the state, especially in [Montgomery](/wiki/Montgomery_County,_Maryland "Montgomery County, Maryland") and [Howard](/wiki/Howard_County,_Maryland "Howard County, Maryland") counties, with large numbers in [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore_County,_Maryland "Baltimore County, Maryland"), [Frederick](/wiki/Frederick_County,_Maryland "Frederick County, Maryland"), and [Prince George's](/wiki/Prince_George%27s_County,_Maryland "Prince George's County, Maryland") counties.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The Indian American population is culturally and linguistically diverse, with the Indian languages spoken most being [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi"), [Gujarati](/wiki/Gujarati_language "Gujarati language"), [Telugu](/wiki/Telugu_language "Telugu language"), and [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] There are also large [Pakistani American](/wiki/Pakistani_Americans "Pakistani Americans") populations throughout the D.C. and Baltimore areas, especially in Baltimore County and Howard County, and a large [Bangladeshi American](/wiki/Bangladeshi_Americans "Bangladeshi Americans") community in the D.C. area.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Maryland has one of the largest populations of [Nepali Americans](/wiki/Nepalese_Americans "Nepalese Americans"), including [Bhutanese Americans](/wiki/Bhutanese_Americans "Bhutanese Americans") of Nepali descent, in the U.S., many of whom are recent immigrants or refugees who sought asylum after [expulsion from Bhutan](/wiki/Lhotshampa "Lhotshampa") or the [2015 Nepal earthquake](/wiki/April_2015_Nepal_earthquake "April 2015 Nepal earthquake"); there are an estimated 20,000 to 25,000 Nepalis in Maryland, concentrated in the Baltimore area with significant populations in the D.C. area.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The first Nepali American elected to a state legislature, [Harry Bhandari](/wiki/Harry_Bhandari "Harry Bhandari"), was elected in Maryland, representing part of Baltimore County.[[152]](#cite_note-155)[[153]](#cite_note-156) There are three [state-recognized tribes](/wiki/State-recognized_tribes_in_the_United_States "State-recognized tribes in the United States"), and in 2020, 31,845 identified as being Native American alone, and 96,805 did in combination with one or more other races.[[154]](#cite_note-157)

Attracting educated Asians and Africans to the professional jobs in the region, Maryland has the fifth-largest proportions of racial minorities in the country.[[155]](#cite_note-158)

In 2006, 645,744 were counted as foreign born, which represents mainly people from Latin America and Asia. About four percent are [undocumented immigrants](/wiki/Undocumented_immigrants "Undocumented immigrants").[[156]](#cite_note-159)

According to The Williams Institute's analysis of the 2010 U.S. census, 12,538 same-sex couples are living in Maryland, representing 5.8 same-sex couples per 1,000 households.[[157]](#cite_note-freedomtomarry.org-160)

[Romani people](/wiki/Romani_people "Romani people") are present in Maryland.[[158]](#cite_note-161)

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Religion")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/BasilicaExterior.jpg/250px-BasilicaExterior.jpg)](/wiki/File:BasilicaExterior.jpg)

[Baltimore Basilica](/wiki/Basilica_of_the_National_Shrine_of_the_Assumption_of_the_Blessed_Virgin_Mary "Basilica of the National Shrine of the Assumption of the Blessed Virgin Mary"), the first [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") [cathedral](/wiki/Cathedral "Cathedral") built in the U.S.

Religion in Maryland (2014)[[159]](#cite_note-Pew_Religion_and_Politics-162)

|  |  |  |  |  |

| Religion | |  | Percent | |
| [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") | |  | 52% | |
| [None](/wiki/Irreligion "Irreligion") | |  | 23% | |

Maryland has been historically prominent to [American Catholic tradition](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_the_United_States "Catholic Church in the United States") because the English [colony of Maryland](/wiki/Colony_of_Maryland "Colony of Maryland") was intended by [George Calvert](/wiki/George_Calvert "George Calvert") as a haven for English Catholics. Baltimore was the seat of the first Catholic bishop in the U.S. (1789), and [Emmitsburg](/wiki/Emmitsburg "Emmitsburg") was the home and burial place of the first American-born citizen to be [canonized](/wiki/Canonized "Canonized"), [St. Elizabeth Ann Seton](/wiki/St._Elizabeth_Ann_Seton "St. Elizabeth Ann Seton"). [Georgetown University](/wiki/Georgetown_University "Georgetown University"), the first Catholic University, was founded in 1789 in what was then part of Maryland; it became a part of the District of Columbia when it was created in the 1790s. The [Basilica of the National Shrine of the Assumption of the Virgin Mary](/wiki/Basilica_of_the_National_Shrine_of_the_Assumption_of_the_Virgin_Mary "Basilica of the National Shrine of the Assumption of the Virgin Mary") in Baltimore was the first Roman Catholic cathedral built in the United States, and the [Archbishop of Baltimore](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Baltimore "Archbishop of Baltimore") is, albeit without formal primacy, the United States' quasi-[primate](/wiki/Primate_(bishop) "Primate (bishop)"),[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and often a cardinal. Among the immigrants of the 19th and 20th centuries from eastern and southern Europe were many Catholics.

Despite its historic relevance to the Catholic Church in the United States, the percentage of Catholics in the state of Maryland is below the national average of 20%. Demographically, both Protestants and those identifying with no religion are more numerous than Catholics.

According to the [Pew Research Center](/wiki/Pew_Research_Center "Pew Research Center") in 2014, 69 percent of Maryland's population identifies themselves as [Christian](/wiki/Christians "Christians"). Nearly 52% of the adult population are [Protestants](/wiki/Protestants "Protestants").[[d]](#cite_note-163) Following Protestantism, [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholicism "Catholicism") is the second largest religious affiliation, comprising 15% percent of the population.[[159]](#cite_note-Pew_Religion_and_Politics-162)[[160]](#cite_note-www.thearda.com-164) [Amish](/wiki/Amish "Amish")/[Mennonite](/wiki/Mennonite "Mennonite") communities are found in [St. Mary's](/wiki/St._Mary%27s_County,_Maryland "St. Mary's County, Maryland"), [Garrett](/wiki/Garrett_County,_Maryland "Garrett County, Maryland"), and [Cecil](/wiki/Cecil_County,_Maryland "Cecil County, Maryland") counties.[[161]](#cite_note-amishamerica-165) [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism") is the largest non-Christian religion in Maryland, with 241,000 adherents, or four percent of the total population.[[162]](#cite_note-166) [Jews](/wiki/American_Jews "American Jews") are numerous throughout [Montgomery County](/wiki/Montgomery_County,_Maryland "Montgomery County, Maryland") and in [Pikesville](/wiki/Pikesville,_Maryland "Pikesville, Maryland") and [Owings Mills](/wiki/Owings_Mills "Owings Mills") northwest of Baltimore. An estimated 81,500 Jewish Americans live in Montgomery County, constituting approximately 10% of the total population.[[163]](#cite_note-167) The [Seventh-day Adventist Church](/wiki/Seventh-day_Adventist_Church "Seventh-day Adventist Church")'s world headquarters and [Ahmadiyya](/wiki/Ahmadiyya "Ahmadiyya") Muslims' national headquarters are located in [Silver Spring](/wiki/Silver_Spring,_Maryland "Silver Spring, Maryland"), just outside [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.")

Per the [Public Religion Research Institute](/wiki/Public_Religion_Research_Institute "Public Religion Research Institute") in 2020, 61 percent of Maryland's population identified with Christianity.[[164]](#cite_note-168) Protestantism and Roman Catholicism continued to dominate the Christian landscape, and the Jewish community remained at 3% of the total religious population. Of the unaffiliated, the PRRI study determined their increase to 28% of the population.

### LGBT population

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: LGBT population")]

Main article: [LGBT rights in Maryland](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_Maryland "LGBT rights in Maryland")

In 2023, the [Williams Institute](/wiki/Williams_Institute "Williams Institute") found 5.4% of Marylanders identify as lesbian, gay, bisexual, or transgender, 0.1% below the national average.[[165]](#cite_note-169) Maryland ranks as one of the best states in the nation for rights of the LGBT community,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] with protections against discrimination enacted since 2001 for sexuality and 2014 for gender, same-sex marriage legalization in 2013, bans on conversion therapy enacted in 2018, abolition of the gay panic defense in 2021, and issuance in 2023 of an executive order protecting the rights of transgender individuals. In 2020, Montgomery County unanimously passed an ordinance implementing an LGBTIQ+ bill of rights.

The first person known to describe himself as a [drag queen](/wiki/Drag_queen "Drag queen") was [William Dorsey Swann](/wiki/William_Dorsey_Swann "William Dorsey Swann"), born enslaved in Hancock, Maryland. Swann was the first American on record who pursued legal and political action to defend the [LGBTQ community](/wiki/LGBTQ_community "LGBTQ community")'s [right to assemble](/wiki/Right_to_assemble "Right to assemble").[[166]](#cite_note-:6-170)

In February 2010, Attorney General [Doug Gansler](/wiki/Doug_Gansler "Doug Gansler") issued an opinion stating that Maryland law should honor [same-sex marriages](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage "Same-sex marriage") from out of state. At the time, the state Supreme Court wrote a decision upholding marriage discrimination.[[157]](#cite_note-freedomtomarry.org-160)

On March 1, 2012, Maryland Governor [Martin O'Malley](/wiki/Martin_O%27Malley "Martin O'Malley") signed the freedom to marry bill into law after it passed in the state legislature. Opponents of same-sex marriage began collecting signatures to overturn the law, which faced a referendum, as Question 6, in the November 2012 election.[[157]](#cite_note-freedomtomarry.org-160) A January 2011 Gonzales Research & Marketing Strategies poll showed 51% support for marriage in the state.[[167]](#cite_note-171)

In May 2012, Maryland's Court of Appeals ruled that the state will recognize marriages of same-sex couples who married out-of-state, no matter the outcome of the November election.[[157]](#cite_note-freedomtomarry.org-160) Voters upheld the bill, passing Question 6 with 52% to 48% on November 6, 2012. Same-sex couples began marrying in Maryland on January 1, 2013.[[157]](#cite_note-freedomtomarry.org-160)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Economy")]

See also: [Business in Maryland](/wiki/Business_in_Maryland "Business in Maryland"), [List of federal installations in Maryland](/wiki/List_of_federal_installations_in_Maryland "List of federal installations in Maryland"), [List of shopping malls in Maryland](/wiki/List_of_shopping_malls_in_Maryland "List of shopping malls in Maryland"), and [Maryland locations by per capita income](/wiki/Maryland_locations_by_per_capita_income "Maryland locations by per capita income")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Geo_Map_of_Median_Income_by_Location_in_Maryland.png/250px-Geo_Map_of_Median_Income_by_Location_in_Maryland.png)](/wiki/File:Geo_Map_of_Median_Income_by_Location_in_Maryland.png)

A map showing Maryland's median income by county. Data is sourced from the 2014 [ACS](/wiki/American_Community_Survey "American Community Survey") five-year estimate report, published by the [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau")

The [Bureau of Economic Analysis](/wiki/Bureau_of_Economic_Analysis "Bureau of Economic Analysis") estimates that Maryland's [Gross State Product](/wiki/Gross_regional_domestic_product "Gross regional domestic product") in 2024 was $433 billion.[[168]](#cite_note-172) However, Maryland has been using [Genuine Progress Indicator](/wiki/Genuine_Progress_Indicator "Genuine Progress Indicator"), an indicator of well-being, to guide the state's development, rather than relying only on growth indicators like GDP.[[169]](#cite_note-173)[[170]](#cite_note-174) According to the [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), Maryland households are currently the wealthiest in the country, with a 2013 median household income of $72,483,[[171]](#cite_note-175) which puts it ahead of [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey") and [Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut "Connecticut"), which are second and third respectively. Two of Maryland's counties, [Howard](/wiki/Howard_County,_Maryland "Howard County, Maryland") and [Montgomery](/wiki/Montgomery_County,_Maryland "Montgomery County, Maryland"), are the second and eleventh [wealthiest counties in the nation](/wiki/Highest-income_counties_in_the_United_States "Highest-income counties in the United States") respectively. Montgomery County has the largest economy in Maryland by far; its [Gross Domestic Product](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") (GDP) of more than $117 Billion makes up 25% of the state’s total economy as of 2024.[[172]](#cite_note-176)[[173]](#cite_note-177) Maryland has the most millionaires per capita in 2013, with a ratio of 7.7 percent.[[174]](#cite_note-178) Also, the state's poverty rate of 7.8 percent is the lowest in the country.[[175]](#cite_note-179)[[176]](#cite_note-180)[[177]](#cite_note-181) [Per capita personal income](/wiki/Per_capita_personal_income_in_the_United_States "Per capita personal income in the United States") in 2024 was $78,538.[[178]](#cite_note-182) As of March 2022, the state's unemployment rate was 4.6 percent.[[179]](#cite_note-183)

Maryland's economy benefits from the state's proximity to the federal government in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), with an emphasis on technical and administrative tasks for the defense/aerospace industry and bio-research laboratories, as well as staffing of satellite government headquarters in the suburban or exurban Baltimore/Washington area. Ft. Meade serves as the headquarters of the [Defense Information Systems Agency](/wiki/Defense_Information_Systems_Agency "Defense Information Systems Agency"), [United States Cyber Command](/wiki/United_States_Cyber_Command "United States Cyber Command"), and the [National Security Agency](/wiki/National_Security_Agency "National Security Agency")/[Central Security Service](/wiki/Central_Security_Service "Central Security Service"). In addition, a number of educational and medical research institutions are located in the state. The various components of The [Johns Hopkins University](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_University "Johns Hopkins University") and its medical research facilities are now the largest single employer in the Baltimore area.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Manufacturing, while large in dollar value, is highly diversified with no sub-sector contributing over 20 percent of the total. Typical forms of manufacturing include electronics, computer equipment, and chemicals. The once-mighty primary metals sub-sector, which once included what was then the largest steel factory in the world at [Sparrows Point](/wiki/Sparrows_Point "Sparrows Point"), still exists, but is pressed with foreign competition, [bankruptcies](/wiki/Bankruptcy "Bankruptcy"), and [mergers](/wiki/Mergers_and_acquisitions "Mergers and acquisitions").[[180]](#cite_note-184)[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] During World War II, the Glenn Martin Company (now part of [Lockheed Martin](/wiki/Lockheed_Martin "Lockheed Martin")) airplane factory employed some 40,000 people.

Mining other than construction materials is virtually limited to coal, which is located in the mountainous western part of the state. The brownstone quarries in the east, which gave Baltimore and Washington much of their characteristic architecture in the mid-19th century, were once a predominant natural resource. Historically, there used to be small gold-mining operations in Maryland, some near Washington, but these no longer exist.

In 2022, the top private employers by number of employees were [BYK Gardner](/wiki/BYK_Additives_%26_Instruments "BYK Additives & Instruments"), [Clean Harbors](/wiki/Clean_Harbors "Clean Harbors"), [Holy Cross Hospital](/wiki/Holy_Cross_Hospital_(Silver_Spring) "Holy Cross Hospital (Silver Spring)"), [Johns Hopkins Bayview Medical Center](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_Bayview_Medical_Center "Johns Hopkins Bayview Medical Center"), [Johns Hopkins University Applied Physics Laboratory](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_University_Applied_Physics_Laboratory "Johns Hopkins University Applied Physics Laboratory"), [Johns Hopkins University School of Medicine](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_University_School_of_Medicine "Johns Hopkins University School of Medicine"), [Maryland Neuroimaging Center](/wiki/University_of_Maryland "University of Maryland"), [Northrop Grumman](/wiki/Northrop_Grumman "Northrop Grumman"), [University of Maryland](/wiki/University_System_of_Maryland "University System of Maryland"), and [University of Maryland Medical Center](/wiki/University_of_Maryland_Medical_Center "University of Maryland Medical Center").[[181]](#cite_note-185)

In 2025, 99.6% of businesses in Maryland were [small businesses](/wiki/Small_business "Small business"), and employed 47.9% of the state's workforce.[[182]](#cite_note-186)

### Port of Baltimore

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Port of Baltimore")]

One major service activity is transportation, centered on the [Port of Baltimore](/wiki/Port_of_Baltimore "Port of Baltimore") and its related [rail](/wiki/Railroad "Railroad") and trucking access. The port ranked 17th in the U.S. by tonnage in 2008.[[183]](#cite_note-187) Although the port handles a wide variety of products, the most typical imports are raw materials and bulk commodities, such as [iron ore](/wiki/Iron_ore "Iron ore"), [petroleum](/wiki/Petroleum "Petroleum"), [sugar](/wiki/Sugar "Sugar"), and [fertilizers](/wiki/Fertilizer "Fertilizer"), often distributed to the relatively close manufacturing centers of the inland [Midwest](/wiki/Midwest "Midwest") via good overland transportation. The port also receives several brands of imported motor vehicles and is the number one auto port in the U.S.[[184]](#cite_note-188)

Baltimore City is among the top 15 largest ports in the nation,[[185]](#cite_note-189) and was one of six major U.S. ports that were part of the February 2006 [controversy](/wiki/Dubai_Ports_World_controversy "Dubai Ports World controversy") over the [Dubai Ports World](/wiki/Dubai_Ports_World "Dubai Ports World") deal.[[186]](#cite_note-190) The state as a whole is heavily industrialized, with a prosperous economy and influential technology centers. Its computer industries are some of the most sophisticated in the United States, and the federal government has invested heavily in the area. Maryland is home to several large military bases and scores of high-level government jobs.

The [Chesapeake and Delaware Canal](/wiki/Chesapeake_and_Delaware_Canal "Chesapeake and Delaware Canal") is a 14 miles (23 km) [canal](/wiki/Canal "Canal") on the Eastern Shore that connects the waters of the Delaware River with those of the Chesapeake Bay, and in particular with the Port of Baltimore, carrying 40 percent of the port's ship traffic.[[187]](#cite_note-191)

### Fishing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Fishing")]

Maryland has a large food-production sector. A large component of this is commercial [fishing](/wiki/List_of_freshwater_fishes_of_Maryland "List of freshwater fishes of Maryland"), centered in the Chesapeake Bay, but also including activity off the short Atlantic seacoast. The largest catches by species are the [blue crab](/wiki/Callinectes_sapidus "Callinectes sapidus"), [oysters](/wiki/Oysters "Oysters"), [striped bass](/wiki/Striped_bass "Striped bass"), and [menhaden](/wiki/Menhaden "Menhaden"). The Bay also has overwintering waterfowl in its wildlife refuges. The waterfowl support a [tourism sector of sportsmen](/wiki/Birdwatching "Birdwatching").

### Agriculture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Agriculture")]

Main article: [Agriculture in Maryland](/wiki/Agriculture_in_Maryland "Agriculture in Maryland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/CarrollCountyMD.jpg/250px-CarrollCountyMD.jpg)](/wiki/File:CarrollCountyMD.jpg)

Agriculture is an important part of Maryland's economy.

Maryland has large areas of fertile agricultural land in its coastal and [Piedmont](/wiki/Piedmont_Plateau "Piedmont Plateau") zones, though this land use is being encroached upon by urbanization. Agriculture is oriented to dairy farming (especially in foothill and piedmont areas) for nearby large city milksheads, plus specialty perishable horticulture crops, such as [cucumbers](/wiki/Cucumber "Cucumber"), [watermelons](/wiki/Watermelon "Watermelon"), [sweet corn](/wiki/Sweet_corn "Sweet corn"), [tomatoes](/wiki/Tomato "Tomato"), [melons](/wiki/Cucumis_melo "Cucumis melo"), [squash](/wiki/Squash_(fruit) "Squash (fruit)"), and [peas](/wiki/Peas "Peas") (Source:USDA Crop Profiles). The southern counties of the western shoreline of Chesapeake Bay are warm enough to support a [tobacco](/wiki/Tobacco "Tobacco") [cash crop](/wiki/Cash_crop "Cash crop") zone, which has existed since early Colonial times, but declined greatly after a state government buy-out in the 1990s. There is also a large automated [chicken](/wiki/Chicken "Chicken")-farming sector in the state's southeastern part; [Salisbury](/wiki/Salisbury,_Maryland "Salisbury, Maryland") is home to [Perdue Farms](/wiki/Perdue_Farms "Perdue Farms"). Maryland's food-processing plants are the most significant type of manufacturing by value in the state. Farming suffers from weeds as anywhere else, including an unusual [multiply resistant](/wiki/Multiple_herbicide_resistance "Multiple herbicide resistance") ragweed (*[Ambrosia artemisiifolia](/wiki/Ambrosia_artemisiifolia "Ambrosia artemisiifolia")*) found by Rousonelos *et al.*, 2012 with both [ALS-](/wiki/ALS_resistance "ALS resistance") and [PPO](/wiki/PPO_resistance "PPO resistance")-resistances[[188]](#cite_note-Rousonelos-et-al-2012-192) and which by 2016 had developed a third, [EPSP resistance](/wiki/EPSP_resistance "EPSP resistance").[[189]](#cite_note-Heap-2022-193) This ragweed population is a drag on [soybean](/wiki/Soybean "Soybean") cultivation and, as of May 7, 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit), is the worst multiresistant weed problem in the state.[[189]](#cite_note-Heap-2022-193)

### Biotechnology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Biotechnology")]

Maryland is a major center for [life sciences](/wiki/Life_sciences "Life sciences") research and development. With more than 400 biotechnology companies located there, Maryland is the fourth largest nexus in this field in the United States.[[190]](#cite_note-194)

Institutions and government agencies with an interest in research and development located in Maryland include the [Johns Hopkins University](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_University "Johns Hopkins University"), the [Johns Hopkins Applied Physics Laboratory](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_Applied_Physics_Laboratory "Johns Hopkins Applied Physics Laboratory"), more than one campus of the [University System of Maryland](/wiki/University_System_of_Maryland "University System of Maryland"), [Goddard Space Flight Center](/wiki/Goddard_Space_Flight_Center "Goddard Space Flight Center"), the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the [National Institutes of Health](/wiki/National_Institutes_of_Health "National Institutes of Health"), the [National Institute of Standards and Technology](/wiki/National_Institute_of_Standards_and_Technology "National Institute of Standards and Technology"), the [National Institute of Mental Health](/wiki/National_Institute_of_Mental_Health "National Institute of Mental Health"), the [Walter Reed National Military Medical Center](/wiki/Walter_Reed_National_Military_Medical_Center "Walter Reed National Military Medical Center"), the federal [Food and Drug Administration](/wiki/Food_and_Drug_Administration "Food and Drug Administration"), the [Howard Hughes Medical Institute](/wiki/Howard_Hughes_Medical_Institute "Howard Hughes Medical Institute"), the [Celera Genomics](/wiki/Celera_Genomics "Celera Genomics") company, the [J. Craig Venter Institute](/wiki/J._Craig_Venter_Institute "J. Craig Venter Institute"), and [AstraZeneca](/wiki/AstraZeneca "AstraZeneca") (formerly [MedImmune](/wiki/MedImmune "MedImmune")).

Maryland is home to defense contractor [Emergent BioSolutions](/wiki/Emergent_BioSolutions "Emergent BioSolutions"), which manufactures and provides an [anthrax vaccine](/wiki/Anthrax_vaccine "Anthrax vaccine") to U.S. government military personnel.[[191]](#cite_note-195)

### Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Tourism")]

See also: [List of National Historic Landmarks in Maryland](/wiki/List_of_National_Historic_Landmarks_in_Maryland "List of National Historic Landmarks in Maryland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Ocean_City_MD_beach_looking_north_at_25th_Street.jpeg/250px-Ocean_City_MD_beach_looking_north_at_25th_Street.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Ocean_City_MD_beach_looking_north_at_25th_Street.jpeg)

[Ocean City](/wiki/Ocean_City,_Maryland "Ocean City, Maryland"), a beach resort along the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") and a popular tourist destination in Maryland

Tourism is popular in Maryland. Many tourists visit Baltimore, the beaches of the Eastern Shore, and the nature of western Maryland. Attractions in Baltimore include the [Harborplace](/wiki/Harborplace "Harborplace"), the [Baltimore Aquarium](/wiki/Baltimore_Aquarium "Baltimore Aquarium"), [Fort McHenry](/wiki/Fort_McHenry "Fort McHenry"), as well as the [Camden Yards](/wiki/Camden_Yards "Camden Yards") baseball stadium. [Ocean City](/wiki/Ocean_City,_Maryland "Ocean City, Maryland") on the Atlantic Coast has been a popular beach destination in summer, particularly since the [Chesapeake Bay Bridge](/wiki/Chesapeake_Bay_Bridge "Chesapeake Bay Bridge") was built in 1952 connecting the Eastern Shore to the more populated Maryland cities.[[46]](#cite_note-baybridge.com-history-48) The state capital of [Annapolis](/wiki/Annapolis "Annapolis") offers sites such as the [state capitol building](/wiki/Maryland_State_House "Maryland State House"), the [historic district](/wiki/Colonial_Annapolis_Historic_District "Colonial Annapolis Historic District"), and the waterfront. Maryland also has several sites of interest to military history, given Maryland's role in the [American Civil War](/wiki/Maryland_in_the_American_Civil_War "Maryland in the American Civil War") and in the [War of 1812](/wiki/Battle_of_Baltimore "Battle of Baltimore"). Other attractions include the historic and picturesque towns along the [Chesapeake Bay](/wiki/Chesapeake_Bay "Chesapeake Bay"), such as [Saint Mary's](/wiki/St._Mary%27s_City,_Maryland "St. Mary's City, Maryland"), Maryland's first colonial settlement and original capital.[[192]](#cite_note-darkroom.baltimoresun.com-196)

### Healthcare

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Healthcare")]

Main articles: [List of hospitals in Maryland](/wiki/List_of_hospitals_in_Maryland "List of hospitals in Maryland") and [Maryland hospital payment system](/wiki/Maryland_hospital_payment_system "Maryland hospital payment system")

As of 2017, the top two health insurers including all types of insurance were [CareFirst BlueCross BlueShield](/wiki/CareFirst_BlueCross_BlueShield "CareFirst BlueCross BlueShield") with 47% market share followed by [UnitedHealth Group](/wiki/UnitedHealth_Group "UnitedHealth Group") at 15%.[[193]](#cite_note-197)

Maryland has experimented with healthcare payment reforms, notably beginning in the 1970s with an [all-payer rate setting](/wiki/All-payer_rate_setting "All-payer rate setting") program regulated by the Health Services Cost Review Commission.[[194]](#cite_note-:3-198) In 2014, it switched to a global budget revenue system, whereby hospitals receive a [capitated](/wiki/Capitation_(healthcare) "Capitation (healthcare)") payment to care for their population.[[194]](#cite_note-:3-198)

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Transportation")]

The [Maryland Department of Transportation](/wiki/Maryland_Department_of_Transportation "Maryland Department of Transportation") oversees most transportation in the state through its various administration-level agencies.[[195]](#cite_note-199) The independent [Maryland Transportation Authority](/wiki/Maryland_Transportation_Authority "Maryland Transportation Authority") maintains and operates the state's eight toll facilities.

### Roads

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Roads")]

See also: [List of Interstate Highways in Maryland](/wiki/List_of_Interstate_Highways_in_Maryland "List of Interstate Highways in Maryland"), [List of Maryland state highways](/wiki/List_of_Maryland_state_highways "List of Maryland state highways"), [List of minor Maryland state highways](/wiki/List_of_minor_Maryland_state_highways "List of minor Maryland state highways"), and [List of former Maryland state highways](/wiki/List_of_former_Maryland_state_highways "List of former Maryland state highways")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Chesapeake_Bay_Bridge_viewed_from_Sandy_Point_State_Park.jpg/250px-Chesapeake_Bay_Bridge_viewed_from_Sandy_Point_State_Park.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chesapeake_Bay_Bridge_viewed_from_Sandy_Point_State_Park.jpg)

The [Chesapeake Bay Bridge](/wiki/Chesapeake_Bay_Bridge "Chesapeake Bay Bridge"), which connects Maryland's [Eastern](/wiki/Eastern_Shore_of_Maryland "Eastern Shore of Maryland") and Western Shores

Maryland's [Interstate highways](/wiki/Interstate_Highway_System "Interstate Highway System") include 110 miles (180 km) of [I-95](/wiki/Interstate_95_in_Maryland "Interstate 95 in Maryland"), which enters the northeast portion of the state, travels through [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore"), and becomes part of the eastern section of the [Capital Beltway](/wiki/Capital_Beltway "Capital Beltway") to the [Woodrow Wilson Bridge](/wiki/Woodrow_Wilson_Bridge "Woodrow Wilson Bridge"). [I-68](/wiki/Interstate_68#Maryland "Interstate 68") travels 81 miles (130 km), connecting the western portions of the state to [I-70](/wiki/Interstate_70_in_Maryland "Interstate 70 in Maryland") in the small town of Hancock. I-70 enters from Pennsylvania north of Hancock and continues east for 93 miles (150 km) to Baltimore, connecting [Hagerstown](/wiki/Hagerstown,_Maryland "Hagerstown, Maryland") and [Frederick](/wiki/Frederick,_Maryland "Frederick, Maryland") along the way.

[I-83](/wiki/Interstate_83#Maryland "Interstate 83") has 34 miles (55 km) in Maryland and connects Baltimore to southern central Pennsylvania ([Harrisburg](/wiki/Harrisburg,_Pennsylvania "Harrisburg, Pennsylvania") and [York, Pennsylvania](/wiki/York,_Pennsylvania "York, Pennsylvania")). Maryland also has an 11-mile (18 km) portion of [I-81](/wiki/Interstate_81_in_Maryland "Interstate 81 in Maryland") that travels through the state near Hagerstown. [I-97](/wiki/Interstate_97 "Interstate 97"), fully contained within Anne Arundel County and the shortest (17.6 miles (28.3 km)) one- or two-digit interstate highway in the contiguous US, connects the Baltimore area to the Annapolis area.

There are also several [auxiliary Interstate highways](/wiki/List_of_auxiliary_Interstate_Highways "List of auxiliary Interstate Highways") in Maryland. Among them are two beltways encircling the major cities of the region: [I-695](/wiki/Interstate_695_(Maryland) "Interstate 695 (Maryland)"), the McKeldin (Baltimore) Beltway, which encircles Baltimore; and a portion of [I-495](/wiki/Capital_Beltway "Capital Beltway"), the Capital Beltway, which encircles [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.") [I-270](/wiki/Interstate_270_(Maryland) "Interstate 270 (Maryland)"), which connects the Frederick area with [Northern Virginia](/wiki/Northern_Virginia "Northern Virginia") and Washington, D.C. through major suburbs to the northwest of Washington, is a major commuter route and is as wide as fourteen lanes at points. [I-895](/wiki/Interstate_895 "Interstate 895"), also known as the Baltimore Harbor Tunnel Thruway, provides an alternate route to I-95 across [Baltimore Harbor](/wiki/Baltimore_Harbor "Baltimore Harbor").

Both I-270 and the Capital Beltway were extremely [congested](/wiki/Traffic_congestion "Traffic congestion"); however, the [Intercounty Connector](/wiki/Maryland_Route_200 "Maryland Route 200") has alleviated some congestion over time. Construction of the ICC was a major part of the campaign platform of former Governor [Robert Ehrlich](/wiki/Robert_Ehrlich "Robert Ehrlich"), who was in office from 2003 until 2007, and of Governor [Martin O'Malley](/wiki/Martin_O%27Malley "Martin O'Malley"), who succeeded him. [I-595](/wiki/U.S._Route_50_in_Maryland "U.S. Route 50 in Maryland"), which is an [unsigned highway](/wiki/Unsigned_highway "Unsigned highway") concurrent with [US 50](/wiki/U.S._Route_50_in_Maryland "U.S. Route 50 in Maryland")/[US 301](/wiki/U.S._Route_301_in_Maryland "U.S. Route 301 in Maryland"), is the longest unsigned interstate in the country and connects [Prince George's County](/wiki/Prince_George%27s_County,_Maryland "Prince George's County, Maryland") and Washington, D.C. with [Annapolis](/wiki/Annapolis,_Maryland "Annapolis, Maryland") and the [Eastern Shore](/wiki/Eastern_Shore_of_Maryland "Eastern Shore of Maryland") via the [Chesapeake Bay Bridge](/wiki/Chesapeake_Bay_Bridge "Chesapeake Bay Bridge").

Maryland also has a [state highway](/wiki/State_highway "State highway") system that contains routes numbered from 2 through 999, however most of the higher-numbered routes are either unsigned or are relatively short. Major state highways include Routes [2](/wiki/Maryland_Route_2 "Maryland Route 2") (Governor Ritchie Highway/Solomons Island Road/Southern Maryland Blvd.), [4](/wiki/Maryland_Route_4 "Maryland Route 4") ([Pennsylvania Avenue](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Avenue_(Washington,_D.C.) "Pennsylvania Avenue (Washington, D.C.)")/Southern Maryland Blvd./Patuxent Beach Road/St. Andrew's Church Road), [5](/wiki/Maryland_Route_5 "Maryland Route 5") (Branch Avenue/Leonardtown Road/Point Lookout Road), [32](/wiki/Maryland_Route_32 "Maryland Route 32"), [45](/wiki/Maryland_Route_45 "Maryland Route 45") (York Road), [97](/wiki/Maryland_Route_97 "Maryland Route 97") (Georgia Avenue), [100](/wiki/Maryland_Route_100 "Maryland Route 100") (Paul T. Pitcher Memorial Highway), [210](/wiki/Maryland_Route_210 "Maryland Route 210") (Indian Head Highway), [235](/wiki/Maryland_Route_235 "Maryland Route 235") (Three Notch Road), [295](/wiki/Maryland_Route_295 "Maryland Route 295") (Baltimore-Washington Parkway), [355](/wiki/Maryland_Route_355 "Maryland Route 355") (Wisconsin Avenue/Rockville Pike/Frederick Road), [404](/wiki/Maryland_Route_404 "Maryland Route 404") (Queen Anne Highway/ Shore Highway), and [650](/wiki/Maryland_Route_650 "Maryland Route 650") (New Hampshire Avenue).

The Maryland Department of Transportation reported 480 fatalities in Maryland for 2025, the first recording below 500 since 2014.[[196]](#cite_note-200)

### Airports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Airports")]

See also: [Aviation in Maryland](/wiki/Aviation_in_Maryland "Aviation in Maryland") and [List of airports in Maryland](/wiki/List_of_airports_in_Maryland "List of airports in Maryland")

Maryland's largest airport is [Baltimore-Washington International Thurgood Marshall Airport](/wiki/Baltimore/Washington_International_Airport "Baltimore/Washington International Airport"), more commonly referred to as BWI. The airport is named for the Baltimore-born [Thurgood Marshall](/wiki/Thurgood_Marshall "Thurgood Marshall"), the first African-American [Supreme Court justice](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States"). The only other airports with commercial service are at [Hagerstown](/wiki/Hagerstown_Regional_Airport "Hagerstown Regional Airport") and [Salisbury](/wiki/Wicomico_Regional_Airport "Wicomico Regional Airport").

The Maryland suburbs of Washington, D.C. are also served by the other two airports in the region, [Ronald Reagan Washington National Airport](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan_Washington_National_Airport "Ronald Reagan Washington National Airport") and [Dulles International Airport](/wiki/Dulles_International_Airport "Dulles International Airport"), both in [Northern Virginia](/wiki/Northern_Virginia "Northern Virginia"). The [College Park Airport](/wiki/College_Park_Airport "College Park Airport") is the nation's oldest, founded in 1909, and is still used. [Wilbur Wright](/wiki/Wilbur_Wright "Wilbur Wright") trained military aviators at this location.[[197]](#cite_note-201)[[198]](#cite_note-202)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Ellicott_City_Station_1970.jpg/250px-Ellicott_City_Station_1970.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ellicott_City_Station_1970.jpg)

[Ellicott City Station](/wiki/Ellicott_City_Station "Ellicott City Station"), on the original [Baltimore and Ohio Railroad](/wiki/Baltimore_and_Ohio_Railroad "Baltimore and Ohio Railroad") line, the oldest remaining passenger station in the nation. The rail line is still used by [CSX Transportation](/wiki/CSX_Transportation "CSX Transportation") for freight trains, and the station is now a museum.

[Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak") trains, including the high-speed [Acela Express](/wiki/Acela_Express "Acela Express") serve [Penn Station](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Station_(Baltimore) "Pennsylvania Station (Baltimore)") in [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore"), [BWI Airport](/wiki/BWI_Rail_Station "BWI Rail Station"), [New Carrollton](/wiki/New_Carrollton_(Washington_Metro) "New Carrollton (Washington Metro)"), and [Aberdeen](/wiki/Aberdeen_(Amtrak_station) "Aberdeen (Amtrak station)") along the [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.") to [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston") [Northeast Corridor](/wiki/Northeast_Corridor "Northeast Corridor"). Train service is provided to [Rockville](/wiki/Rockville,_Maryland "Rockville, Maryland") and [Cumberland](/wiki/Cumberland,_Maryland "Cumberland, Maryland") by [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak")'s Washington, D.C., to [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") [Capitol Limited](/wiki/Capitol_Limited_(Amtrak) "Capitol Limited (Amtrak)").

The [WMATA](/wiki/WMATA "WMATA")'s [Metrorail](/wiki/Washington_Metro "Washington Metro") [rapid transit](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") and [Metrobus](/wiki/Metrobus_(Washington,_D.C.) "Metrobus (Washington, D.C.)") local bus systems (the [2nd](/wiki/List_of_United_States_rapid_transit_systems_by_ridership "List of United States rapid transit systems by ridership") and [6th](/wiki/List_of_United_States_local_bus_agencies_by_ridership "List of United States local bus agencies by ridership") busiest in the nation of their respective modes) provide service in Montgomery and Prince George's counties and connect them to Washington D.C.. The [Maryland Transit Administration](/wiki/Maryland_Transit_Administration "Maryland Transit Administration") (often abbreviated as "MTA Maryland"), a state agency part of the [Maryland Department of Transportation](/wiki/Maryland_Department_of_Transportation "Maryland Department of Transportation") also provides transit services within the state. Headquartered in Baltimore, MTA's transit services are largely focused on central Maryland, as well as some portions of the Eastern Shore and Southern MD. Baltimore's [Light RailLink](/wiki/Baltimore_Light_RailLink "Baltimore Light RailLink") and [Metro SubwayLink](/wiki/Baltimore_Metro_SubwayLink "Baltimore Metro SubwayLink") systems serve its densely populated inner-city and the surrounding suburbs. The MTA also serves the city and its suburbs with its [local bus](/wiki/MTA_Maryland_bus_service "MTA Maryland bus service") service (the [9th largest system](/wiki/List_of_United_States_local_bus_agencies_by_ridership "List of United States local bus agencies by ridership") in the nation). The MTA's [Commuter Bus](/wiki/Maryland_Transit_Administration#Commuter_bus "Maryland Transit Administration") system provides express coach service on longer routes connecting Washington, D.C. and Baltimore to parts of Central and Southern MD as well as the Eastern Shore. The commuter rail service, known as [MARC](/wiki/Maryland_Area_Rail_Commuter "Maryland Area Rail Commuter"), operates three lines which all terminate at [Washington Union Station](/wiki/Washington_Union_Station "Washington Union Station") and provide service to Baltimore's [Penn](/wiki/Baltimore_Penn_Station "Baltimore Penn Station") and [Camden](/wiki/Camden_Station "Camden Station") stations, [Perryville](/wiki/Perryville,_Maryland "Perryville, Maryland"), [Frederick](/wiki/Frederick,_Maryland "Frederick, Maryland"), and [Martinsburg, West Virginia](/wiki/Martinsburg,_West_Virginia "Martinsburg, West Virginia"). In addition, many suburban counties operate local bus systems which connect to and complement the larger MTA and WMATA/Metro services.

The MTA will also administer the [Purple Line](/wiki/Purple_Line_(Maryland) "Purple Line (Maryland)"), an under-construction light rail line that will connect the Maryland branches of the [Red](/wiki/Red_Line_(Washington_Metro) "Red Line (Washington Metro)"), [Green](/wiki/Green_Line_(Washington_Metro) "Green Line (Washington Metro)")/[Yellow](/wiki/Yellow_Line_(Washington_Metro) "Yellow Line (Washington Metro)"), and [Orange](/wiki/Orange_Line_(Washington_Metro) "Orange Line (Washington Metro)") lines of the Washington Metro, as well as offer transfers to all three lines of the MARC commuter rail system.[[199]](#cite_note-203)[[200]](#cite_note-204)

[Freight rail transport](/wiki/Freight_rail_transport "Freight rail transport") is handled principally by two [Class I railroads](/wiki/Class_I_railroad "Class I railroad"), as well as several smaller regional and local carriers. [CSX Transportation](/wiki/CSX_Transportation "CSX Transportation") has more extensive [trackage](/wiki/Rail_tracks "Rail tracks") throughout the state, with 560 miles (900 km),[[201]](#cite_note-CSXT-205) followed by [Norfolk Southern Railway](/wiki/Norfolk_Southern_Railway "Norfolk Southern Railway"). Major [rail yards](/wiki/Rail_yard "Rail yard") are located in Baltimore and Cumberland,[[201]](#cite_note-CSXT-205) with an [intermodal](/wiki/Intermodal_freight_transport "Intermodal freight transport") terminal (rail, truck and marine) in Baltimore.[[202]](#cite_note-206)

## Law and government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Law and government")]

Main article: [Government of Maryland](/wiki/Government_of_Maryland "Government of Maryland")

See also: [List of Governors of Maryland](/wiki/List_of_Governors_of_Maryland "List of Governors of Maryland"), [Maryland Army National Guard](/wiki/Maryland_Army_National_Guard "Maryland Army National Guard"), and [Maryland Air National Guard](/wiki/Maryland_Air_National_Guard "Maryland Air National Guard")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Annapolis2.jpg/250px-Annapolis2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Annapolis2.jpg)

The [Maryland State House](/wiki/Maryland_State_House "Maryland State House") in [Annapolis](/wiki/Annapolis,_Maryland "Annapolis, Maryland") dates to 1772, and houses the Maryland General Assembly and offices of the [governor](/wiki/Governor_of_Maryland "Governor of Maryland").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Maryland_state_coat_of_arms_%28illustrated%2C_1876%29.jpg/250px-Maryland_state_coat_of_arms_%28illustrated%2C_1876%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maryland_state_coat_of_arms_(illustrated,_1876).jpg)

The [historical coat of arms](/wiki/Historical_coats_of_arms_of_the_U.S._states "Historical coats of arms of the U.S. states") of Maryland in 1876

The [government of Maryland](/wiki/Government_of_Maryland "Government of Maryland") is conducted according to the [state constitution](/wiki/Maryland_Constitution "Maryland Constitution"). The government of Maryland, like the other 49 [state governments](/wiki/Politics_of_the_United_States#Local_government "Politics of the United States"), has exclusive authority over matters that lie entirely within the state's borders, except as limited by the [Constitution of the United States](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_United_States "Constitution of the United States").

Power in Maryland is divided among three branches of government: [executive](/wiki/Executive_branch "Executive branch"), [legislative](/wiki/Legislative_branch "Legislative branch"), and [judicial](/wiki/Judicial_branch "Judicial branch"). The [Maryland General Assembly](/wiki/Maryland_General_Assembly "Maryland General Assembly") is composed of the [Maryland House of Delegates](/wiki/Maryland_House_of_Delegates "Maryland House of Delegates") and the [Maryland Senate](/wiki/Maryland_Senate "Maryland Senate"). [Maryland's governor](/wiki/Governor_of_Maryland "Governor of Maryland") is unique in the United States as the office is vested with significant authority in budgeting. Unlike many other states, significant autonomy is granted to many of Maryland's [counties](/wiki/County_(United_States) "County (United States)").

Most of the business of government is conducted in [Annapolis](/wiki/Annapolis,_Maryland "Annapolis, Maryland"), the state [capital](/wiki/Capital_(political) "Capital (political)"); however some cabinet departments and state officials have their offices in [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore"). Elections for governor and most statewide offices, as well as most county elections, are held in [midterm-election years](/wiki/United_States_midterm_election "United States midterm election") (even-numbered years not divisible by four).

The judicial branch of state government consists of one united [District Court of Maryland](/wiki/Maryland_District_Court "Maryland District Court") that sits in every county and Baltimore City, as well as 24 [Circuit Courts](/wiki/Maryland_Circuit_Courts "Maryland Circuit Courts") sitting in each County and Baltimore City, the latter being courts of general jurisdiction for all civil disputes over $30,000, all equitable jurisdiction and major criminal proceedings. The intermediate appellate court is known as the [Appellate Court of Maryland](/wiki/Appellate_Court_of_Maryland "Appellate Court of Maryland") and the [state supreme court](/wiki/State_supreme_court "State supreme court") is the [Supreme Court of Maryland](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Maryland "Supreme Court of Maryland"). The appearance of the justices of the Supreme Court of Maryland is unique; Maryland is the only state whose justices wear red robes.[[203]](#cite_note-207)

### Taxation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Taxation")]

Maryland imposes five [income tax](/wiki/Income_tax "Income tax") brackets, ranging from 2 to 6.25 percent of personal income.[[204]](#cite_note-208) The city of Baltimore and Maryland's 23 counties levy local "piggyback" income taxes at rates between 1.25 and 3.2 percent of Maryland taxable income. Local officials set the rates and the revenue is returned to the local governments quarterly. The top income tax bracket of 9.45 percent is the fifth highest combined state and local income tax rates in the country, behind New York City's 11.35 percent, California's 10.3 percent, Rhode Island's 9.9 percent, and Vermont's 9.5 percent.[[205]](#cite_note-209)

Maryland's state [sales tax](/wiki/Sales_tax "Sales tax") is six percent.[[206]](#cite_note-210) All real property in Maryland is subject to the [property tax](/wiki/Property_tax "Property tax").[[207]](#cite_note-dat.maryland.gov-211) Generally, properties that are owned and used by religious, charitable, or educational organizations or property owned by the federal, state or local governments are exempt.[[207]](#cite_note-dat.maryland.gov-211) Property tax rates vary widely.[[207]](#cite_note-dat.maryland.gov-211) No restrictions or limitations on property taxes are imposed by the state, meaning cities and counties can set tax rates at the level they deem necessary to fund governmental services.[[207]](#cite_note-dat.maryland.gov-211)

### Elections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Elections")]

Main article: [Elections in Maryland](/wiki/Elections_in_Maryland "Elections in Maryland")

Further information: [Political party strength in Maryland](/wiki/Political_party_strength_in_Maryland "Political party strength in Maryland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Spiro_Agnew.jpg/250px-Spiro_Agnew.jpg)](/wiki/File:Spiro_Agnew.jpg)

[Spiro Agnew](/wiki/Spiro_Agnew "Spiro Agnew"), the 39th [Vice President of the United States](/wiki/Vice_President_of_the_United_States "Vice President of the United States") during the [Nixon administration](/wiki/Presidency_of_Richard_Nixon "Presidency of Richard Nixon"), the highest-ranking political leader from Maryland since the nation's founding

Following the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), Maryland's elections have [largely been controlled](/wiki/Political_party_strength_in_Maryland "Political party strength in Maryland") by the [Democratic Party](/wiki/U.S._Democratic_Party "U.S. Democratic Party"), which accounted for 54.9% of the state's registered voters as of May 2017.[[208]](#cite_note-Maryland_State_Board_of_Elections-212)

State elections are dominated by [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore") and four populous suburban counties bordering [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), and Baltimore: [Montgomery](/wiki/Montgomery_County,_Maryland "Montgomery County, Maryland"), [Prince George's](/wiki/Prince_George%27s_County,_Maryland "Prince George's County, Maryland"), [Anne Arundel](/wiki/Anne_Arundel_County,_Maryland "Anne Arundel County, Maryland"), and [Baltimore counties](/wiki/Baltimore_County "Baltimore County"). As of July 2017,[[209]](#cite_note-213) 66 percent of the state's population resides in these six jurisdictions, most of which contain large, traditionally Democratic [voting blocs](/wiki/Voting_bloc "Voting bloc"): [African Americans](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans") in Baltimore City and Prince George's; [federal](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States") employees in Prince George's, Anne Arundel, and Montgomery; and [post-graduates](/wiki/Postgraduate_education "Postgraduate education") in Montgomery. The remainder of the state, particularly [Western Maryland](/wiki/Western_Maryland "Western Maryland") and the [Eastern Shore](/wiki/Eastern_Shore_of_Maryland "Eastern Shore of Maryland"), is more supportive of [Republicans](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] One of Maryland's best known political figures is a Republican – former governor [Spiro Agnew](/wiki/Spiro_Agnew "Spiro Agnew"), who pled [no contest](/wiki/Nolo_contendere "Nolo contendere") to tax evasion and resigned in 1973.[[210]](#cite_note-214)

In [1980](/wiki/1980_United_States_presidential_election "1980 United States presidential election"), Maryland was one of six states to vote for [Jimmy Carter](/wiki/Jimmy_Carter "Jimmy Carter"). In [1992](/wiki/1992_United_States_presidential_election "1992 United States presidential election"), [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton") fared better in Maryland than any other state, except his home state of [Arkansas](/wiki/Arkansas "Arkansas"). In [1996](/wiki/1996_United_States_presidential_election "1996 United States presidential election"), Maryland was Clinton's sixth best; in [2000](/wiki/2000_United_States_presidential_election "2000 United States presidential election"), Maryland ranked fourth for Gore; and in [2004](/wiki/2004_United_States_presidential_election "2004 United States presidential election"), [John Kerry](/wiki/John_Kerry "John Kerry") showed his fifth-best performance in Maryland. In [2008](/wiki/2008_United_States_presidential_election "2008 United States presidential election"), [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") won the state's 10 electoral votes with 61.9 percent of the vote, to [John McCain](/wiki/John_McCain "John McCain")'s 36.5 percent.

In 2002, former Governor [Robert Ehrlich](/wiki/Robert_Ehrlich "Robert Ehrlich") was the first Republican to be elected to that office in four decades, and after one term, he lost his seat to [Baltimore Mayor](/wiki/Baltimore_Mayor "Baltimore Mayor") and Democrat [Martin O'Malley](/wiki/Martin_O%27Malley "Martin O'Malley"). Ehrlich ran again for governor in 2010, losing again to O'Malley.

Voter registration and party enrollment in Maryland  
as of August 2025[[211]](#cite_note-215)

| Party | | Total | Percentage |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | [Democratic](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") | 2,232,476 | 51.67% |
|  | [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") | 1,030,610 | 23.86% |
