The 2006 election brought no change in the pattern of Democratic dominance. After Democratic Senator [Paul Sarbanes](/wiki/Paul_Sarbanes "Paul Sarbanes") announced that he was retiring, Democratic Congressman [Benjamin Cardin](/wiki/Benjamin_Cardin "Benjamin Cardin") defeated Republican Lieutenant Governor [Michael S. Steele](/wiki/Michael_S._Steele "Michael S. Steele"), with 55 percent of the vote, against Steele's 44 percent.

While Republicans typically win more counties in statewide elections by piling up large margins in the west and east, they are usually overcome by the densely populated and solidly Democratic Baltimore–Washington axis. In [2008](/wiki/2008_United_States_presidential_election_in_Maryland "2008 United States presidential election in Maryland"), for instance, McCain won 17 counties to Obama's six (plus Baltimore City). While McCain won most of the western and eastern counties by margins of 2-to-1 or more, he was almost completely shut out in the larger counties surrounding Baltimore and Washington; every large county except Anne Arundel went for Obama, who won by 25 points statewide.[[212]](#cite_note-216)

From 2007 to 2011, U.S. Congressman [Steny Hoyer](/wiki/Steny_Hoyer "Steny Hoyer") ([MD-5](/wiki/Maryland%27s_5th_congressional_district "Maryland's 5th congressional district")), a [Democrat](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)"), was elected as [Majority Leader](/wiki/Majority_Leader "Majority Leader") for the [110th Congress](/wiki/110th_United_States_Congress "110th United States Congress") and [111th Congress](/wiki/111th_United_States_Congress "111th United States Congress") of the [House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives"), serving in that post again from 2019 to 2023. In addition, Hoyer served as House Minority Whip from 2003 to 2006 and 2012 to 2018. His district covers parts of [Anne Arundel](/wiki/Anne_Arundel_County,_Maryland "Anne Arundel County, Maryland") and [Prince George's](/wiki/Prince_George%27s_County,_Maryland "Prince George's County, Maryland") counties, in addition to all of [Charles](/wiki/Charles_County,_Maryland "Charles County, Maryland"), [Calvert](/wiki/Calvert_County,_Maryland "Calvert County, Maryland"), and [St. Mary's](/wiki/Saint_Mary%27s_County,_Maryland "Saint Mary's County, Maryland") counties in [southern Maryland](/wiki/Southern_Maryland "Southern Maryland").[[213]](#cite_note-217)

In 2010, Republicans won control of most counties. The Democratic Party remained in control of eight county governments, including that of [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore").[[214]](#cite_note-218)

In [2022](/wiki/2022_Maryland_gubernatorial_election "2022 Maryland gubernatorial election"), [Wes Moore](/wiki/Wes_Moore "Wes Moore") replaced Republican [Larry Hogan](/wiki/Larry_Hogan "Larry Hogan"), who did not run for re-election due to term limits. Moore is the first African-American elected Governor of Maryland, and the fifth African-American governor in American history.[[215]](#cite_note-219)

### Immigration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Immigration")]

In 2025, Maryland passed several pieces of legislation designed to counter [immigration-related executive orders from President Trump](/wiki/Immigration_policy_of_the_second_Trump_administration "Immigration policy of the second Trump administration"), among them being the "Protecting Sensitive Locations Act" which limits access of [Immigration and Customs Enforcement](/wiki/United_States_Immigration_and_Customs_Enforcement "United States Immigration and Customs Enforcement") (ICE) to locales such as schools and health facilities.[[216]](#cite_note-220)

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Media")]

A well-known newspaper in Maryland is *[The Baltimore Sun](/wiki/The_Baltimore_Sun "The Baltimore Sun")*.[[217]](#cite_note-221)
Many residents of the [Washington metropolitan area](/wiki/Washington_metropolitan_area "Washington metropolitan area") receive *[The Washington Post](/wiki/The_Washington_Post "The Washington Post").*[[218]](#cite_note-222)

The most populous areas are served by either [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore") or [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.") broadcast stations. The Eastern Shore is served primarily by [broadcast media](/wiki/Broadcasting "Broadcasting") based around the [Delmarva Peninsula](/wiki/Delmarva_Peninsula "Delmarva Peninsula"); the northeastern section receives both Baltimore and [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") stations. [Garrett County](/wiki/Garrett_County,_Maryland "Garrett County, Maryland"), which is mountainous, is served by stations from [Pittsburgh](/wiki/Pittsburgh "Pittsburgh"), and requires cable or satellite for reception. Maryland is served by statewide [PBS](/wiki/PBS "PBS") member station [Maryland Public Television](/wiki/Maryland_Public_Television "Maryland Public Television") (MPT).[[219]](#cite_note-223)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Education")]

### Primary and secondary education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Primary and secondary education")]

See also: [List of school districts in Maryland](/wiki/List_of_school_districts_in_Maryland "List of school districts in Maryland"), [List of high schools in Maryland](/wiki/List_of_high_schools_in_Maryland "List of high schools in Maryland"), and [Arts and culture of Maryland](/wiki/Arts_and_culture_of_Maryland "Arts and culture of Maryland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Memorial_Chapel_at_UMCP%2C_front_view_off-center%2C_August_21%2C_2006.jpg/250px-Memorial_Chapel_at_UMCP%2C_front_view_off-center%2C_August_21%2C_2006.jpg)](/wiki/File:Memorial_Chapel_at_UMCP,_front_view_off-center,_August_21,_2006.jpg)

Memorial Chapel at the [University of Maryland](/wiki/University_of_Maryland,_College_Park "University of Maryland, College Park"), Maryland's flagship university

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/UMBC_Commons_and_Quad.jpg/250px-UMBC_Commons_and_Quad.jpg)](/wiki/File:UMBC_Commons_and_Quad.jpg)

[University of Maryland, Baltimore County](/wiki/University_of_Maryland,_Baltimore_County "University of Maryland, Baltimore County")

*Education Week* ranked Maryland #1 in its nationwide 2009–2013 Quality Counts reports.[[220]](#cite_note-224) Primary and secondary education in Maryland is overseen by the [Maryland State Department of Education](/wiki/Maryland_State_Department_of_Education "Maryland State Department of Education"), which is headquartered in [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore,_Maryland "Baltimore, Maryland").[[221]](#cite_note-225) The highest educational official in the state is the [State Superintendent of Schools](/wiki/Superintendent_(education) "Superintendent (education)"), who is appointed by the [State Board of Education](/wiki/Board_of_education "Board of education") to a four-year term of office. The Maryland General Assembly has given the Superintendent and State Board autonomy to make educationally related decisions, limiting its influence on the day-to-day functions of public education. Each county and county-equivalent in Maryland has a [local Board of Education](/wiki/List_of_school_districts_in_Maryland "List of school districts in Maryland") charged with running the public schools in that particular jurisdiction.

The budget for education was $5.5 billion in 2009, representing about 40 percent of the state's general fund.[[222]](#cite_note-226) Data from the 2017 census shows that, among large school districts, four Maryland districts are in the top six for per-pupil annual spending, exceeded only by the Boston and New York City districts.[[223]](#cite_note-227)

Maryland has a broad range of private primary and secondary schools. Many of these are affiliated with various religious sects, including [parochial schools](/wiki/Parochial_schools "Parochial schools") of the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), [Quaker](/wiki/Quaker "Quaker") schools, [Seventh-day Adventist](/wiki/Seventh-day_Adventist_Church "Seventh-day Adventist Church") schools, and [Jewish](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism") schools. In 2003, Maryland law was changed to allow for the creation of publicly funded charter schools, although the charter schools must be approved by their local Board of Education and are not exempt from state laws on education, including collective bargaining laws.

In 2008, the state led the entire country in the percentage of students passing [Advanced Placement](/wiki/Advanced_Placement "Advanced Placement") examinations. 23.4 percent of students earned passing grades on the AP tests given in May 2008. This marks the first year that Maryland earned this honor.[[224]](#cite_note-228) Three Maryland high schools (in Montgomery County) were ranked among the top 100 in the country by US News in 2009, based in large part on AP test scores.[[225]](#cite_note-229)

### Colleges and universities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Colleges and universities")]

See also: [List of colleges and universities in Maryland](/wiki/List_of_colleges_and_universities_in_Maryland "List of colleges and universities in Maryland")

Maryland has several historic and renowned private colleges and universities, the most prominent of which is [Johns Hopkins University](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_University "Johns Hopkins University"), founded in 1876 with a grant from Baltimore entrepreneur [Johns Hopkins](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins "Johns Hopkins").

The first public university in the state is the [University of Maryland, Baltimore](/wiki/University_of_Maryland,_Baltimore "University of Maryland, Baltimore"), which was founded in 1807 and contains the University of Maryland's only public academic [health](/wiki/University_of_Maryland_School_of_Medicine "University of Maryland School of Medicine"), human services, and one of two [law centers](/wiki/University_of_Maryland_School_of_Law "University of Maryland School of Law") (the other being the [University of Baltimore School of Law](/wiki/University_of_Baltimore_School_of_Law "University of Baltimore School of Law")). Seven professional and graduate schools train the majority of the state's physicians, nurses, dentists, lawyers, social workers, and pharmacists.[[226]](#cite_note-230) The flagship university and largest undergraduate institution in Maryland is the [University of Maryland, College Park](/wiki/University_of_Maryland,_College_Park "University of Maryland, College Park") which was founded as the Maryland Agricultural College in 1856 and became a public [land grant college](/wiki/Land_grant_college "Land grant college") in 1864. [Towson University](/wiki/Towson_University "Towson University"), founded in 1866, is the state's second largest university.

In 1974, Maryland, along with seven other states, mainly in the South, submitted plans to desegregate its state universities; Maryland's plans were approved by the U.S. Department of Health, Education and Welfare.[[227]](#cite_note-231)

Baltimore is home to the [University of Maryland, Baltimore County](/wiki/University_of_Maryland,_Baltimore_County "University of Maryland, Baltimore County") and the [Maryland Institute College of Art](/wiki/Maryland_Institute_College_of_Art "Maryland Institute College of Art"). The majority of public universities in the state (Bowie State University, Coppin State University, Frostburg State University, Salisbury University and the University of Maryland-Eastern Shore) are affiliated with the [University System of Maryland](/wiki/University_System_of_Maryland "University System of Maryland"). Two state-funded institutions, [Morgan State University](/wiki/Morgan_State_University "Morgan State University") and [St. Mary's College of Maryland](/wiki/St._Mary%27s_College_of_Maryland "St. Mary's College of Maryland"), as well as two federally funded institutions, the [Uniformed Services University of the Health Sciences](/wiki/Uniformed_Services_University_of_the_Health_Sciences "Uniformed Services University of the Health Sciences") and the [United States Naval Academy](/wiki/United_States_Naval_Academy "United States Naval Academy"), are not affiliated with the University System of Maryland. The [University of Maryland Global Campus](/wiki/University_of_Maryland_Global_Campus "University of Maryland Global Campus") is the largest public university in Maryland[[228]](#cite_note-232) and one of the largest distance-learning institutions in the world.[[229]](#cite_note-233)

[St. John's College](/wiki/St._John%27s_College_(Annapolis/Santa_Fe) "St. John's College (Annapolis/Santa Fe)") in [Annapolis](/wiki/Annapolis,_Maryland "Annapolis, Maryland") and [Washington College](/wiki/Washington_College "Washington College") in [Chestertown](/wiki/Chestertown,_Maryland "Chestertown, Maryland"), both private institutions, are the oldest colleges in the state and among the oldest in the country. Other private institutions include [Mount St. Mary's University](/wiki/Mount_St._Mary%27s_University_(Maryland) "Mount St. Mary's University (Maryland)"), [McDaniel College](/wiki/McDaniel_College "McDaniel College") (formerly known as Western Maryland College), [Hood College](/wiki/Hood_College "Hood College"), [Stevenson University](/wiki/Stevenson_University "Stevenson University") (formerly known as Villa Julie College), [Loyola University Maryland](/wiki/Loyola_University_Maryland "Loyola University Maryland"), and [Goucher College](/wiki/Goucher_College "Goucher College"), among others.

### Education assessments and impact

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Education assessments and impact")]

There are several state-level assessments which are used to characterize schools and changes in their educational efficacy. The annual Maryland School Report Card which scores districts and individual schools based on graduation rates, attendance information and academic achievement among other factors.[[230]](#cite_note-:5-234) The Maryland Comprehensive Assessment Program (MCAP) is an assessment of individual students in English and math, beginning in the 3rd grade.[[230]](#cite_note-:5-234) As of 2027, the MCAP will begin to be used to determine whether 3rd grade students would need to repeat the grade level or could proceed to the 4th grade.[[230]](#cite_note-:5-234)

### Public libraries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Public libraries")]

Maryland's 24 public library systems deliver public education for everyone in the state of Maryland through a curriculum that comprises three pillars: Self-Directed Education (books and materials in all formats, e-resources), Research Assistance & Instruction (individualized research assistance, classes for students of all ages), and Instructive & Enlightening Experiences (e.g., Summer Reading Clubs, author events).

Maryland's library systems include, in part:

* [Anne Arundel County Public Library](/wiki/Anne_Arundel_County_Public_Library "Anne Arundel County Public Library")
* [Baltimore County Public Library](/wiki/Baltimore_County_Public_Library "Baltimore County Public Library")
* [Cecil County Public Library](/wiki/Cecil_County_Public_Library "Cecil County Public Library")
* [Enoch Pratt Free Library](/wiki/Enoch_Pratt_Free_Library "Enoch Pratt Free Library")
* Frederick County Public Library
* [Harford County Public Library](/wiki/Harford_County_Public_Library "Harford County Public Library")
* [Howard County Public Library](/wiki/Howard_County_Public_Library "Howard County Public Library")
* [Montgomery County Public Libraries](/wiki/Montgomery_County_Public_Libraries "Montgomery County Public Libraries")

## Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maryland&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Sports")]

See also: [Sports in Maryland](/wiki/Sports_in_Maryland "Sports in Maryland") and [List of people from Maryland § Athletes](/wiki/List_of_people_from_Maryland#Athletes "List of people from Maryland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/CamdenYards_2005-05-08.jpg/250px-CamdenYards_2005-05-08.jpg)](/wiki/File:CamdenYards_2005-05-08.jpg)

[Oriole Park at Camden Yards](/wiki/Oriole_Park_at_Camden_Yards "Oriole Park at Camden Yards"), home of the [Baltimore Orioles](/wiki/Baltimore_Orioles "Baltimore Orioles")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/M%26T_Bank_Stadium_DoD.jpg/250px-M%26T_Bank_Stadium_DoD.jpg)](/wiki/File:M%26T_Bank_Stadium_DoD.jpg)

[M&T Bank Stadium](/wiki/M%26T_Bank_Stadium "M&T Bank Stadium"), home of the [Baltimore Ravens](/wiki/Baltimore_Ravens "Baltimore Ravens")

With two major metropolitan areas, Maryland has a number of major and minor professional sports franchises. Two [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") teams play in Maryland, the [Baltimore Ravens](/wiki/Baltimore_Ravens "Baltimore Ravens") in [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore,_Maryland "Baltimore, Maryland") and the [Washington Commanders](/wiki/Washington_Commanders "Washington Commanders") in [Landover](/wiki/Landover,_Maryland "Landover, Maryland"). The [Baltimore Colts](/wiki/History_of_the_Baltimore_Colts "History of the Baltimore Colts") represented the NFL in Baltimore from 1953 to 1983 before moving to [Indianapolis](/wiki/Indianapolis "Indianapolis").

The [Baltimore Orioles](/wiki/Baltimore_Orioles "Baltimore Orioles") are the state's [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") franchise. The [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League")'s [Washington Capitals](/wiki/Washington_Capitals "Washington Capitals") and the [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association")'s [Washington Wizards](/wiki/Washington_Wizards "Washington Wizards") formerly played in Maryland, until the construction of an arena in Washington, D.C. in 1997 (now known as [Capital One Arena](/wiki/Capital_One_Arena "Capital One Arena")). [University of Maryland](/wiki/University_of_Maryland,_College_Park "University of Maryland, College Park")'s team is the [Maryland Terrapins](/wiki/Maryland_Terrapins "Maryland Terrapins").

Maryland enjoys considerable historical repute for the talented sports players of its past, including [Cal Ripken Jr.](/wiki/Cal_Ripken_Jr. "Cal Ripken Jr.") and [Babe Ruth](/wiki/Babe_Ruth "Babe Ruth"). In 2012, *[The Baltimore Sun](/wiki/The_Baltimore_Sun "The Baltimore Sun")* published a list of Maryland's top ten athletes in the state's history. The list includes Babe Ruth, Cal Ripken Jr, [Johnny Unitas](/wiki/Johnny_Unitas "Johnny Unitas"), [Brooks Robinson](/wiki/Brooks_Robinson "Brooks Robinson"), [Frank Robinson](/wiki/Frank_Robinson "Frank Robinson"), [Ray Lewis](/wiki/Ray_Lewis "Ray Lewis"), [Michael Phelps](/wiki/Michael_Phelps "Michael Phelps"), [Jimmie Foxx](/wiki/Jimmie_Foxx "Jimmie Foxx"), [Jim Parker](/wiki/Jim_Parker_(American_football) "Jim Parker (American football)"), and [Wes Unseld](/wiki/Wes_Unseld "Wes Unseld").[[233]](#cite_note-237)

Other professional sports franchises in the state include three affiliated [minor league baseball](/wiki/Minor_league_baseball "Minor league baseball") teams, one [independent league baseball](/wiki/Independent_league_baseball "Independent league baseball") team, the [Baltimore Blast](/wiki/Baltimore_Blast_(current) "Baltimore Blast (current)") indoor soccer team, two indoor football teams and three low-level outdoor soccer teams. Maryland is also home to one of the three races in horse racing's annual Triple Crown, the [Preakness Stakes](/wiki/Preakness_Stakes "Preakness Stakes"), which is run every spring at [Pimlico Race Course](/wiki/Pimlico_Race_Course "Pimlico Race Course") in Baltimore. [The Baltimore Stallions](/wiki/Baltimore_Stallions "Baltimore Stallions") were a Canadian Football Team in the [CFL](/wiki/Canadian_Football_League "Canadian Football League") that played the 1994–95 season.

[Congressional Country Club](/wiki/Congressional_Country_Club "Congressional Country Club") has hosted three golf tournaments for the [U.S. Open](/wiki/U.S._Open_(golf) "U.S. Open (golf)") and a [PGA Championship](/wiki/PGA_Championship "PGA Championship").

The official state sport of Maryland, since 1962, is [jousting](/wiki/Jousting "Jousting"); the official team sport since 2004 is [lacrosse](/wiki/Lacrosse "Lacrosse").[[234]](#cite_note-238) The [National Lacrosse Hall of Fame](/wiki/National_Lacrosse_Hall_of_Fame "National Lacrosse Hall of Fame") is located in [Sparks, Maryland](/wiki/Sparks,_Maryland "Sparks, Maryland") at the [USA Lacrosse](/wiki/US_Lacrosse "US Lacrosse") headquarters. In 2008, intending to promote physical fitness for all ages, [walking](/wiki/Walking#Leisure_activity "Walking") became the official state exercise. Maryland is the first state with an official state exercise.[[235]](#cite_note-239)

66. **[^](#cite_ref-68)** 
    Therres, Glenn (Fall 2007). ["Lions in our mountains? The mystery of cougars in Maryland"](https://web.archive.org/web/20110419203123/http://www.dnr.state.md.us/naturalresource/fall2007/lions.pdf) (PDF). *Wildlife and Heritage*. Maryland Department of Natural Resources. Archived from [the original](http://www.dnr.state.md.us/naturalresource/fall2007/lions.pdf) (PDF) on April 19, 2011. Retrieved July 6, 2009. "Historically bobcats were distributed statewide but during the post colonization period densities began to plummet. By the mid-1900s, populations had probably reached all-time lows, with remnant populations existing only in western Maryland. This prompted the Department of Natural Resources (DNR) to classify them as a state-listed "Species of Special Concern." During the past quarter century, occupied range and densities have increased markedly. Results from the annual Bowhunter Survey and the Hunter Mail survey have identified bobcat sightings in 14 of Maryland's 23 counties. Currently, bobcats have dual legal classification in Maryland. In addition to the Species of Special Concern designation, they are also defined as a Game Animal / Furbearer with a closed harvest season."
67. **[^](#cite_ref-69)** 
    ["Coyotes in Maryland"](https://web.archive.org/web/20110815030808/http://www.dnr.state.md.us/wildlife/Hunt_Trap/furbearers/coyote.asp). Maryland Department of Natural Resources. Archived from [the original](http://www.dnr.state.md.us/wildlife/Hunt_Trap/furbearers/coyote.asp) on August 15, 2011. Retrieved September 16, 2011. "Coyotes were historically a western species with core populations found west of the Mississippi River. Alterations and/or elimination of competing predators during the post-European colonization period facilitated rapid range expansion into eastern North America during the 20th Century. Established populations now occur in every state and province in North America. Coyotes are a relatively new addition to local ecosystems, and were first documented in Maryland during 1972. Initial substantiated sightings occurred in Cecil, Frederick and Washington counties. Since that time population densities and occupied range have expanded incrementally and coyotes now occur statewide."
